AI.tldr: Bit-ViP is a bit-plane-based image obfuscation scheme that uses Lorenz chaotic system and differential privacy to produce secure, non-invertible, yet trainable images for visual privacy in recognition tasks, validated on UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets.
AI.motivation: Computer vision applications (surveillance, social media) raise security and visual privacy concerns when data is stored on cloud servers. Existing obfuscation schemes are either vulnerable to model inversion attacks or generate non-trainable images with poor accuracy.
AI.method: A bit-plane-based obfuscation scheme (Bit-ViP) with an end-to-end obfuscation function that incorporates non-invertible noise from Lorenz's chaotic system and differential privacy to prevent image reconstruction.
AI.result: Validated on UCF101 and HMDB51 datasets; rigorous security analysis shows improvements over existing schemes against reconstruction attacks, pixel frequency, information entropy, and pixel inter-correlation attacks.
AI.conclusion: Bit-ViP effectively balances visual privacy preservation and usability (trainability) for image-based recognition tasks, outperforming prior obfuscation methods in security.

title: Robust Zero-shot Anomaly Detection under Limited Auxiliary Anomaly Priors
summary: Zero-shot anomaly detection aims to identify defects in arbitrary novel domains; however, existing models assume that the auxiliary data contains a rich diversity of anomalies, neglecting the far more complex and unpredictable variations in real-world target domains. This study introduces DIVE, the first approach to investigate the scenario of limited auxiliary anomaly priors and resolve the resulting substantial performance degradation. Through a shallow-and-deep text embedding injection strategy during visual encoding, DIVE learns to abstract generic anomaly concepts shared across the auxiliary training domain and diverse target domains. Moreover, we propose a disentanglement mechanism to tackle the suboptimal alignment between visual embeddings entangled with object semantics and object-agnostic textual prompts. Experiments demonstrate that, under the setting of limited anomaly patterns in auxiliary data, DIVE outperforms SOTA baselines by up to 16.2% and 28.5% on two classification metrics, and 23.4%, 24.1%, and 47.0% on three segmentation metrics, in terms of average performance across twelve datasets. Furthermore, it maintains highly competitive performance when auxiliary data exhibits sufficient anomaly diversity.

AI.tldr: DIVE is the first zero-shot anomaly detection method designed for scenarios with limited auxiliary anomaly priors, using a shallow-and-deep text embedding injection strategy and a disentanglement mechanism to improve alignment, outperforming SOTA baselines by up to 47% across twelve datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing zero-shot anomaly detection models assume auxiliary data contains rich anomaly diversity, but real-world target domains exhibit far more complex and unpredictable variations. This paper addresses the performance degradation when limited anomaly priors are available.
AI.method: DIVE uses a shallow-and-deep text embedding injection strategy during visual encoding to learn abstract generic anomaly concepts, plus a disentanglement mechanism to resolve suboptimal alignment between visual embeddings (entangled with object semantics) and object-agnostic textual prompts.
AI.result: Under limited anomaly patterns in auxiliary data, DIVE outperforms SOTA by up to 16.2% and 28.5% on classification metrics, and 23.4%, 24.1%, and 47.0% on segmentation metrics across twelve datasets. It also maintains competitive performance with sufficient anomaly diversity.
AI.conclusion: DIVE effectively tackles zero-shot anomaly detection under limited anomaly priors and remains competitive when anomaly diversity is sufficient, demonstrating robustness across diverse settings.

title: EvLIR: Learning Illumination Residuals from Ordered Events for Low-Light Image Enhancement
summary: Low-light image enhancement is severely ill-posed when the input frame contains missing structure, saturated noise, and weak local contrast. Event cameras provide asynchronous brightness-change observations with high temporal resolution, but prior works often treat voxel channels as an unordered or static feature stack before fusion, rather than explicitly modeling their within-window temporal evolution, weakening the temporal evidence that makes events useful. We propose EvLIR, a temporal-residual enhancement framework that learns illumination residuals from ordered events for low-light image enhancement. Given a low-light frame and its aligned event voxel, EvLIR preserves the ordered temporal bins of the event stream and introduces a Temporal Event Residual Module (TERM) to encode short-window event dynamics with a lightweight ConvGRU. The resulting temporal state is converted into a bounded illumination correction, which provides spatially adaptive photometric guidance for Retinex-style illumination estimation and subsequent reliability-aware image-event restoration. On SDE and SDSD indoor/outdoor benchmarks, EvLIR achieves the best result on eleven of twelve dataset-metric pairs, with average scores of 25.63~dB PSNR, 28.30~dB PSNR*, and 0.827 SSIM across the four benchmarks.

AI.tldr: EvLIR is a temporal-residual framework that uses ordered event camera data and a ConvGRU module to enhance low-light images, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Low-light image enhancement is ill-posed due to missing structure, saturated noise, and weak contrast. Prior event-based methods treat event voxel channels as unordered/static features, ignoring temporal evolution within event windows, thus weakening the useful temporal evidence from events.
AI.method: EvLIR preserves ordered temporal bins of event streams and introduces a Temporal Event Residual Module (TERM) using lightweight ConvGRU to encode short-window event dynamics. The temporal state is converted into bounded illumination correction for spatially adaptive Retinex-style illumination estimation and reliability-aware image-event restoration.
AI.result: On SDE and SDSD indoor/outdoor benchmarks, EvLIR achieves best results on 11/12 dataset-metric pairs, with average scores of 25.63 dB PSNR, 28.30 dB PSNR*, and 0.827 SSIM across four benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling temporal evolution of events (rather than treating them as static stacks) significantly improves low-light image enhancement, as demonstrated by EvLIR's state-of-the-art performance.

title: Bridging VideoQA and Video-Guided Agentic Tasks via Generalized Keyframe Extraction
comment: Accepted by ECCV 2026. Project Page: https://vg-gui-tasker.github.io/
summary: Video understanding is a fundamental capability for multimodal intelligence, and recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance on Video Question Answering (VideoQA) benchmarks. However, existing benchmarks primarily evaluate whether models can perceive shallow visual cues, while rarely examining whether MLLMs can learn deeper knowledge or procedural skills from video tutorials and generalize them to downstream long-horizon agentic tasks. To address this gap, we introduce VG-GUIBench (Video-Guided GUI Benchmark), a new benchmark designed to evaluate whether MLLM-based GUI agents can follow video tutorials to complete corresponding GUI interactive tasks. Furthermore, we observe that the performance of models on both VideoQA and video-guided agentic tasks critically depends on effective keyframe extraction. Based on this observation, we propose TASKER (Task-driven And Scene-aware Keyframe searchER), a keyframe extraction algorithm that jointly considers task relevance and scene dynamics to identify informative frames. Experimental results demonstrate that TASKER achieves significant performance improvements on both VideoQA and video-guided agentic task benchmarks, outperforming the best baseline by 2.0% on the EgoSchema fullset and 1.8% on the NExT-QA dataset, respectively. These results further highlight the potential of generalized keyframe extraction methods for video understanding tasks. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/VG-GUI-TASKER/VG-GUI-TASKER.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces VG-GUIBench, a benchmark for evaluating whether MLLM-based GUI agents can follow video tutorials to complete tasks, and proposes TASKER, a keyframe extraction algorithm that improves performance on both VideoQA and video-guided agentic tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing VideoQA benchmarks mainly test shallow visual perception rather than deeper knowledge or procedural skill learning from video tutorials. There is a gap in evaluating whether MLLMs can generalize video-guided knowledge to downstream long-horizon agentic tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose VG-GUIBench, a benchmark for video-guided GUI tasks, and TASKER, a task-driven and scene-aware keyframe extraction algorithm that jointly considers task relevance and scene dynamics to identify informative frames.
AI.result: TASKER outperforms the best baseline by 2.0% on EgoSchema fullset and 1.8% on NExT-QA dataset, showing consistent improvements on both VideoQA and video-guided agentic benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Keyframe extraction is critical for both VideoQA and video-guided agentic tasks, and generalized keyframe extraction methods like TASKER have strong potential for improving video understanding capabilities.

title: Miti360: A Comprehensive Dataset for Improved Reforestation Monitoring
comment: 13 figures, 4 tables, 25 pages (20 excluding references), Under review at Nature Scientific Data
summary: Over the past decade, interest in applying machine learning (ML) to automate forest monitoring has grown significantly. However, existing training datasets are predominantly drawn from North America, Europe, Asia, and Australia, leaving a critical gap in African forestry data. To address this limited geographic diversity, we present Miti360, a comprehensive dataset for reforestation monitoring that comprises high-resolution imagery, ground truth data, and longitudinal weather data. Data collection occurred within a 770-ha reforested section of the Kieni Forest in Kenya between March 2023 and February 2025. Miti360 comprises aerial photos (orthophotos and tiles) with tree bounding box annotations, terrestrial images (single and stereo), and detailed data records including tree biophysical parameters, species, and GPS coordinates, alongside historical weather data. Aerial surveys utilized a DJI Mavic 2 Pro, with imagery stitched via Agisoft Metashape and tiled using ArcGIS Pro, while terrestrial captures used smartphones and custom stereo cameras. Miti360 enables the training of ML systems for tasks such as accelerating tree censuses, matching species to geographical areas, modelling growth based on weather conditions, and developing digital twin frameworks. Models can be trained on Miti360 to address challenges specific to Sub-Saharan Africa, ultimately advancing reforestation monitoring and fostering sustainable forestry practices in underrepresented regions. We demonstrate the utility of this dataset by successfully tracking tree crowns across three years and improving the DeepForest model's box precision and box recall by 12% and 69% respectively through fine-tuning on Miti360.

AI.tldr: Miti360 is a comprehensive reforestation monitoring dataset from Kenya (Kieni Forest), featuring aerial/terrestrial imagery, ground truth, and weather data collected over two years, which improves DeepForest model performance by 12% in precision and 69% in recall.
AI.motivation: Existing ML training datasets for forest monitoring lack geographic diversity, particularly missing African forestry data, which limits model generalizability and hinders reforestation efforts in underrepresented regions like Sub-Saharan Africa.
AI.method: Data collected in a 770-ha reforested section of Kieni Forest, Kenya (March 2023–Feb 2025). Aerial: DJI Mavic 2 Pro → Agisoft Metashape stitched → ArcGIS Pro tiled. Terrestrial: smartphones + custom stereo cameras. Includes orthophotos/tiles with tree bounding boxes, single/stereo images, biophysical parameters, species, GPS coordinates, and historical weather data.
AI.result: Fine-tuning DeepForest on Miti360 improved box precision by 12% and box recall by 69% for tracking tree crowns across three years.
AI.conclusion: Miti360 addresses the Sub-Saharan Africa data gap, enabling ML training for tree censuses, species matching, growth modeling, and digital twin frameworks, ultimately advancing reforestation monitoring in underrepresented regions.

title: The Platonic Defense: Backdoor Defense for Self-Supervised Encoders in the Era of Large Scale Pre-training
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) pretrained models have become a dominant paradigm for visual representation learning, but they are vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing defenses struggle to defend against such attacks in a fully black-box setting because they often require access to labels, attack patterns, or training data. To tackle this issue, we propose a new attack-agnostic, model-agnostic, and modality-agnostic black-box test-time defense paradigm, called \emph{Platonic Representation Defense}. It is inspired by the Platonic Representation Hypothesis, which suggests that large-scale independently trained encoders converge toward compatible projections of the same underlying reality. We formalize this idea as a conditional energy function defined over source representations and a set of reference representations. The energy function is trained for detection through noise-contrastive estimation and for representation purification through denoising score matching. Theoretically, the energy gap between matched and mismatched samples is lower bounded by the mutual information between source and reference representations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on multiple self-supervised encoders and more than 10 attacks. The method can perform both representation detection and purification, and achieves substantial performance gains across multiple attacks. Code is available \href{https://github.com/jsrdcht/Platonic-Representation-Defense}{here}.

AI.tldr: Proposes Platonic Representation Defense, a black-box test-time defense against backdoor attacks on SSL models that uses multiple independently trained encoders' representations to detect and purify poisoned representations without needing labels, attack patterns, or training data.
AI.motivation: SSL pretrained models are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, and existing defenses require labels, attack patterns, or training data, failing in fully black-box settings.
AI.method: Inspired by Platonic Representation Hypothesis (independently trained encoders converge toward compatible projections of the same reality). Defines a conditional energy function over source and reference representations, trained via noise-contrastive estimation for detection and denoising score matching for purification.
AI.result: Effective on multiple self-supervised encoders and 10+ attacks; achieves substantial gains in both detection and purification tasks.
AI.conclusion: The method is attack-agnostic, model-agnostic, and modality-agnostic, providing a robust test-time defense with theoretical guarantees (energy gap bounded by mutual information).

title: Resonant Brane Splatting for Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution
summary: Arbitrary-Scale Super-Resolution (ASR) reconstructs images at continuous magnification factors. Recent methods accelerate inference by replacing computationally heavy implicit neural decoders with explicit 2D Gaussian Splatting (GS). However, since standard Gaussians are smooth low-pass primitives, modeling edges and fine textures requires multiple overlapping, well-aligned splats, which creates severe bottlenecks during rasterization. To address this, we introduce Resonant Brane Splatting (RBS), a feed-forward ASR framework. RBS replaces flat Gaussians with Branes: expressive primitives that emit spatially varying colors to natively model local contrast and complex textures within a single footprint. We achieve this by augmenting the standard Gaussian envelope with internal Gaussian-Hermite modes, assigning a distinct color coefficient to each. The zero-order mode recovers standard GS, while higher-order modes capture high frequencies. We predict Brane parameters directly from low-resolution features. Because Branes provide a mathematically richer formulation than simple Gaussians, far fewer primitives need to overlap to reconstruct a given target pixel. To exploit this, we introduce an efficient fully differentiable rasterizer with a precise culling strategy based on the classical quantum turning point. This allows us to safely skip negligible regions, drastically reducing the rendering overhead. Experiments on standard ASR benchmarks show that RBS improves reconstruction quality over implicit and GS baselines, while achieving superior speed-quality trade-off than prior GS methods.

AI.tldr: RBS replaces flat Gaussian splats with "Branes" (Gaussian-Hermite modes) for arbitrary-scale super-resolution, achieving better quality and speed by reducing overlapping primitives and using a quantum-inspired culling strategy.
AI.motivation: Standard 2D Gaussian Splatting (GS) uses smooth low-pass primitives, so modeling edges and fine textures requires many overlapping splats, creating rasterization bottlenecks.
AI.method: RBS introduces Branes—Gaussians augmented with internal Gaussian-Hermite modes where each mode has a distinct color coefficient. Zero-order recovers standard GS; higher orders capture high frequencies. A differentiable rasterizer with a quantum turning point culling strategy skips negligible regions.
AI.result: RBS improves reconstruction quality over implicit and GS baselines on standard ASR benchmarks, achieving a superior speed-quality trade-off compared to prior GS methods.
AI.conclusion: RBS demonstrates that richer primitive representations (Branes) can reduce primitive overlap, accelerate rasterization, and improve ASR performance, offering a better speed-quality balance.

title: From Phase to Phenomenon: Self-Supervised Learning of Subsurface Scattering with Minimal Phase-shift Inputs
summary: We propose a self-supervised pretraining framework for learning sub-surface scattering (SSS) light transport representations from minimal input. Our method leverages a stereo projector-camera setup that captures only eight high-frequency phase-shift profilometry (PSP) images per view to pretrain an encoder in a multi-view, multi-object setting. We introduce a tailored augmentation strategy for PSP-based SSS data, and show that it significantly outperforms standard ImageNet-style augmentations for SSL pretraining. The pretrained encoder learns generalizable SSS representations that transfer effectively to downstream tasks, including spatially varying relighting and representation evaluation using a kNN classifier. Combined with a decoder, the model reconstructs dense scattering footprint responses, trained using a dedicated cost function that improves accuracy, particularly for anisotropic footprints. Despite using only eight input images per view, our approach generalizes to unseen objects with complex geometry and material properties, achieving high-fidelity reconstructions while requiring orders of magnitude fewer images than prior methods.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised pretraining framework that learns subsurface scattering light transport from just 8 high-frequency PSP images per view, using tailored augmentations and a dedicated cost function to achieve high-fidelity reconstructions with far fewer images than prior methods.
AI.motivation: To enable learning generalizable subsurface scattering representations from minimal input data, reducing the enormous image acquisition burden required by prior methods (orders of magnitude fewer images).
AI.method: (1) Stereo projector-camera setup capturing only 8 high-frequency PSP images per view; (2) Self-supervised pretraining with a tailored augmentation strategy for PSP-based SSS data; (3) Encoder-decoder architecture trained with a dedicated cost function for dense scattering footprint reconstruction.
AI.result: The tailored augmentation strategy significantly outperforms standard ImageNet-style augmentations for SSL pretraining. The model generalizes to unseen objects with complex geometry and materials, achieving high-fidelity reconstructions while requiring orders of magnitude fewer images than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised learning on minimal PSP input, combined with domain-specific augmentations and a specialized cost function, is an effective and data-efficient approach for learning subsurface scattering representations that generalize across diverse objects.

title: MIRROR: Aligning Semantic Relations from Language to Image via Gromov--Wasserstein
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) inherit rich relational priors from their language backbones, yet often fail when asked to apply these relationships in visual contexts. We trace this failure to a structural blind spot: projection-based alignment trains each visual token to carry the right semantics, but never asks whether the relationships between concepts survive the crossing from language to vision. To address this, we propose MIRROR (Mapping Inter-concept Relations from language to visual Representation via Optimal-transport-based Regularization), a geometric regularization framework that transfers relational priors from language to vision by exploiting the rich relational structure encoded in language representations. Specifically, we derive a surrogate loss from the proposed Semi-Inverse Gromov-Wasserstein (SI-GW) problem, an inverse geometric problem that aligns visual representations with language-derived relational priors. We show that this formulation admits a unique closed-form solution that prescribes the ideal visual relational structure implied by language geometry and cross-modal coupling. The structure of the formulation also enables efficient computation, making it applicable to long token sequences. Applying SI-GW inside decoder-only Transformers requires careful design. We introduce targeted strategies at the layer, head, and token levels to ensure stable extraction without additional parameters or inference cost. MIRROR improves relational consistency while preserving performance on general vision-language tasks.

AI.tldr: MIRROR introduces a geometric regularization (SI-GW) to transfer relational priors from language to visual representations in MLLMs, fixing the blind spot where projection-based alignment loses inter-concept relationships during cross-modal transfer.
AI.motivation: MLLMs inherit rich relational knowledge from language backbones but fail to apply these relationships in visual contexts because projection-based alignment only ensures individual visual tokens carry correct semantics, ignoring whether inter-concept relationships survive the language-to-vision transfer.
AI.method: Proposes MIRROR, using Semi-Inverse Gromov-Wasserstein (SI-GW) regularization—a geometric framework with a closed-form solution that aligns visual representations with language-derived relational priors. Includes layer-, head-, and token-level strategies for stable integration into decoder-only Transformers without extra parameters or inference cost.
AI.result: MIRROR improves relational consistency in visual understanding while preserving performance on general vision-language tasks, with efficient computation applicable to long token sequences.
AI.conclusion: The SI-GW framework effectively transfers relational structure from language to vision, addressing a fundamental blind spot in MLLM alignment without adding computational overhead or sacrificing general task performance.

title: CellDETR: A Detection-Guided Framework for Scalable Cell Representation Learning from Histopathology Images
summary: Recent advances in pathology foundation models have substantially improved patch and slide level representation learning from whole-slide images (WSIs).However, cell-level representations learning remain underexplored, limiting cell resolved interpretability, biological discovery, and clinical translation. We propose CellDETR, a detection-guided framework built on Deformable DETR for scalable cell representation learning from WSIs. By introducing location feature decoupling and box-constrained attention mechanism, CellDETR enables automated extraction of cell-level embeddings, and outperform existing state-of-the-art methods in supervised cell classification on PanNuke data. In addition, by incorporating contrastive learning design, we build a CellDETR-based pretraining model for scalable cell representation learning from unlabeled WSIs, which improves downstream cell classification performance. Furthermore, we show that after pretraining with Xenium spatial transcriptomics-derived cell annotations, CellDETR achieves accurate cross-dataset cell classification, demonstrating the transferability and biological relevance of the learned cell embeddings. Together, CellDETR provides a scalable route toward general cell-level representation learning framework for interpretable computational patholog

AI.tldr: CellDETR is a detection-guided framework built on Deformable DETR for scalable cell representation learning from whole-slide images, achieving state-of-the-art cell classification and demonstrating transferability across datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing pathology foundation models focus on patch and slide-level representations but neglect cell-level representation learning, limiting interpretability, biological discovery, and clinical translation.
AI.method: CellDETR employs Deformable DETR with location feature decoupling and box-constrained attention, plus contrastive learning for scalable pretraining from unlabeled WSIs and spatial transcriptomics-derived annotations.
AI.result: Outperforms SOTA in supervised cell classification on PanNuke; pretraining improves downstream classification; cross-dataset transfer shows biological relevance.
AI.conclusion: CellDETR offers a scalable route toward general cell-level representation learning for interpretable computational pathology.

title: Rank-Aware Hyperbolic Alignment for Vision-Language Dataset Distillation
comment: Accepted for publication at ECCV 2026. Project Page: https://andyj1.github.io/raha
summary: Vision-language dataset distillation (VLDD) compresses a large image-text paired dataset into a small set of synthetic pairs that can efficiently train contrastive vision-language models under strict data and compute budgets. Most existing methods match expert trajectories or cross-modal statistics, yet still enforce full-dimensional alignment in a Euclidean embedding space. This is often overly restrictive due to rank-deficient image--text correlation, with shared semantics concentrated in a low-dimensional range and remaining variation spread across a weakly correlated residual subspace. LoRS relaxes alignment at the similarity level by low-rank factorization, but does not explicitly control dominant alignment capacity and structure in the representation space. We thus propose a rank-aware hyperbolic alignment (RAHA) that combines hierarchical geometry with explicit alignment-capacity control. RAHA lifts multimodal representations to hyperbolic space and optimizes distilled pairs with asymmetric objectives that enforce geodesic alignment in the shared range while regularizing the residual subspace to preserve modality-private diversity and improve transfer robustness. Experiments on benchmarks show that RAHA demonstrates competitive cross-modal retrieval and improved transfer indicators under fixed budgets.

AI.tldr: RAHA proposes a rank-aware hyperbolic alignment method for vision-language dataset distillation that uses hierarchical geometry and explicit capacity control to improve cross-modal retrieval and transfer robustness under fixed budgets.
AI.motivation: Existing VLDD methods (e.g., LoRS) enforce full-dimensional alignment in Euclidean space, which is overly restrictive due to rank-deficient image-text correlation. LoRS relaxes alignment via low-rank factorization but fails to explicitly control dominant alignment capacity and structure in the representation space.
AI.method: RAHA lifts multimodal representations to hyperbolic space and uses asymmetric objectives: (1) enforce geodesic alignment in the shared low-dimensional range, (2) regularize the residual subspace to preserve modality-private diversity and improve transfer robustness.
AI.result: Competitive cross-modal retrieval performance and improved transfer indicators under fixed data and compute budgets on benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic geometry with explicit alignment-capacity control is effective for vision-language dataset distillation, outperforming prior Euclidean-based methods.

title: MAVIN: Multi-Shot Audio-Visual Generation with Narrative Control
summary: While recent generative models produce high-fidelity videos, they struggle with the complex narrative control required for coherent multi-shot audio-visual generation. Existing methods suffer from temporal misalignment, limited controllability, and incomplete scripting. In this paper, we propose MAVIN, the first framework for multi-shot audio-visual generation with customized narrative control. To resolve temporal misalignment, we propose boundary-aware attention, which leverages hierarchical captions and boundary-aware token routing to render audio-visual elements within their respective temporal boundaries. To improve the controllability for multi-subject scenarios, we propose ID-aware propagation, utilizing identity embeddings and an identity-aware mask to bind specific identities to consistent visual appearances and vocal timbres. To provide comprehensive audio-visual narratives, we present a multi-agent scripting pipeline to transform free-form user inputs into hierarchical captions. Furthermore, we construct MAVINSet, a multi-shot audio-visual dataset for robust training and evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MAVIN achieves state-of-the-art performance, opening up a new avenue for integrating generative models into professional filmmaking workflows.

AI.tldr: MAVIN is the first framework for multi-shot audio-visual generation with narrative control, using boundary-aware attention, ID-aware propagation, and multi-agent scripting to achieve state-of-the-art performance in coherent film-like content creation.
AI.motivation: Existing generative models produce high-fidelity videos but struggle with complex narrative control required for coherent multi-shot audio-visual generation, suffering from temporal misalignment, limited controllability, and incomplete scripting.
AI.method: 1) Boundary-aware attention with hierarchical captions and token routing for temporal alignment; 2) ID-aware propagation using identity embeddings and masks for consistent appearance/timbre; 3) Multi-agent scripting pipeline for transforming user inputs into hierarchical captions; 4) Construction of MAVINSet dataset.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in multi-shot audio-visual generation, enabling integration into professional filmmaking workflows.
AI.conclusion: MAVIN opens a new avenue for integrating generative models into professional filmmaking by resolving key challenges in narrative control, temporal alignment, and multi-subject consistency.

title: VCS-SLAM: Geometry-Validated Semantic Evidence Fusion for 3D Gaussian SLAM
summary: Visual SLAM performance often deteriorates in complex real-world applications. Semantic 3D Gaussian SLAM commonly fuses 2D semantic priors into a persistent 3D map using uniform optimization weights. However, such priors are not equally reliable in online mapping: occlusions, unsupported semantic boundaries, and ambiguous ray geometry can introduce persistent semantic artifacts into the global Gaussian map. We propose VCS-SLAM, a geometry-validated semantic evidence fusion framework for RGB-D 3D Gaussian SLAM. Instead of treating all semantic observations as uniformly valid supervision, VCS-SLAM evaluates their geometric reliability through visibility consistency, surface-supported boundary evidence, and ray-level conflict uncertainty. The resulting reliability-aware objective suppresses occluded semantic updates, reduces unsupported semantic bleeding, and delays premature label assignment in ambiguous regions. Experiments on Replica demonstrate improved semantic consistency, boundary preservation, and reconstruction quality. Results on ScanNet further show that VCS-SLAM maintains competitive tracking performance under real RGB-D inputs

AI.tldr: VCS-SLAM improves semantic 3D Gaussian SLAM by weighting semantic observations based on geometric reliability (visibility, boundary evidence, ray conflict) rather than treating all priors equally, reducing artifacts and improving map quality.
AI.motivation: Existing semantic 3D Gaussian SLAM methods treat all 2D semantic priors as equally reliable, ignoring that occlusions, unsupported boundaries, and ambiguous ray geometry introduce persistent artifacts in the global map.
AI.method: VCS-SLAM evaluates geometric reliability of each semantic observation via visibility consistency, surface-supported boundary evidence, and ray-level conflict uncertainty, then uses a reliability-aware objective to suppress unreliable updates.
AI.result: On Replica, VCS-SLAM achieves improved semantic consistency, boundary preservation, and reconstruction quality. On ScanNet, it maintains competitive tracking performance under real RGB-D inputs.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-validated semantic evidence fusion effectively mitigates semantic artifacts in 3D Gaussian SLAM without sacrificing tracking performance.

title: Rectifying Mask via Entropy for Distractor-Free 3DGS in Ambiguous Scenarios
summary: We present RefineSplat, a systematic framework that effectively constructs transient masks to identify diverse ambiguous distractors. To do this, we qualitatively and quantitatively analyze issues and propose a novel entropy-aware adaptive masking method. Unlike existing approaches that struggle to distinguish transient elements from static scenes due to color or semantic ambiguity, RefineSplat captures ambiguous distractors leveraging entropy and instance masks. Furthermore, we propose a simple yet effective entropy-aware density control to align Gaussians in ambiguous scenarios considering Entropy-aware positional gradients. Additionally, to rigorously validate our method, we first create and release the Ambiguous wild dataset, including 18 scenes where distractors and static scenes are hard to distinguish due to color or semantic resemblances. Experimental results on various datasets demonstrate that RefineSplat shows state-of-the-art performance, showing distractor-free novel view synthesis.

AI.tldr: RefineSplat is a framework that uses entropy-aware adaptive masking and density control to remove transient distractors from 3D Gaussian splatting, achieving state-of-the-art novel view synthesis on a new Ambiguous wild dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle to distinguish transient elements from static scenes when distractors share color or semantic resemblance with the background. The authors aim to build a robust system for identifying diverse ambiguous distractors in novel view synthesis.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) entropy-aware adaptive masking that leverages entropy and instance masks to capture ambiguous distractors, and (2) entropy-aware density control for aligning Gaussians using entropy-weighted positional gradients.
AI.result: RefineSplat achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple datasets, including the newly introduced Ambiguous wild dataset (18 challenging scenes). It successfully produces distractor-free novel view synthesis.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-aware techniques are effective for handling color and semantic ambiguity in transient distractors, and the proposed framework outperforms prior approaches in ambiguous scenarios.

title: Learning Where and When: Patch-Based Spatiotemporal Localization in Weakly Supervised Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Weakly supervised video anomaly detection (WSVAD) has predominantly focused on temporal localization, identifying when anomalies occur while largely neglecting their spatial extent within frames. Yet, spatial localization is essential for interpretability and practical deployment in real-world settings. We introduce a patch-based spatiotemporal framework for weakly supervised anomaly localization that jointly models where and when anomalies occur. Our approach operates on grid-level patch features and learns region-level anomaly scores under a multiple instance learning paradigm. We further propose a Proximity-Aware Top-k spatiotemporal selection strategy that enables the model to generate fine-grained spatial anomaly maps without requiring bounding-box supervision during training. Our method surpasses existing state-of-the-art approaches across multiple benchmarks, yielding substantial gains in spatiotemporal localization accuracy. In addition, we release frame-level bounding-box annotations for the test sets of two widely used datasets, along with our code and pretrained models, providing new resources to facilitate future research in spatially grounded WSVAD.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a patch-based spatiotemporal framework for weakly supervised video anomaly detection that jointly localizes anomalies in both space (where) and time (when), using a Proximity-Aware Top-k selection strategy to generate fine-grained spatial anomaly maps without bounding-box supervision, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing WSVAD methods focus only on temporal localization of anomalies, ignoring their spatial extent within frames. Spatial localization is critical for interpretability and real-world deployment.
AI.method: A patch-based spatiotemporal framework with grid-level patch features and region-level anomaly scores under MIL paradigm, plus a Proximity-Aware Top-k spatiotemporal selection strategy for fine-grained spatial anomaly maps without bounding-box supervision.
AI.result: Outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods across multiple benchmarks with substantial gains in spatiotemporal localization accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively bridges the gap between temporal and spatial anomaly localization in WSVAD, and the release of new annotations and models will support future research in this direction.

title: Empirical Evaluation of Multi-Modal Touch Detection in Over-the-Shoulder Video Surveillance
summary: Video Intelligence Surveillance (VIDINT) on over-the-shoulder footage is a proposed vector for monitoring human-computer interaction patterns without direct screen recording access. In this paper, we evaluate a Behavioral Intelligence (BEHINT) touch-detection framework designed to reconstruct keystroke events on mobile keypad interfaces from physical finger interactions. Our system integrates four parallel detection modalities: (1) anatomical hand landmarks via MediaPipe, (2) HSV skin color filtering, (3) temporal frame differencing for motion detection, and (4) shape-guided Canny edge analysis. We map relative touch coordinates to a reference screen layout to reconstruct typing sequences. Evaluation on a 120-frame first-person staged video of passcode entry reveals that while MediaPipe and Skin Detection fail to run autonomously due to partial hand occlusion and ambient noise, Motion-Only and Edge-Only configurations achieve F1-scores of 18.5% and 18.2%, respectively. The combined multi-modal configuration achieves an F1-score of 16.7% and a sequence similarity of 3.0% when mapped to the iOS passcode layout. We conduct ablation, resolution decay, noise sensitivity, and proximity threshold tuning to characterize the system's operational envelope. We then audit generalization on 5 real, publicly licensed third-person phone videos and find that the detector emits a median of 57 touch points per frame (peaking at 205), one to three orders of magnitude more than the rate of real taps, because the skin filter responds to the whole hand rather than to fingertip contact. The staged keystroke result does not survive contact with uncontrolled footage; the system does not achieve reliable keystroke reconstruction outside the calibrated staged setting.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a BEHINT touch-detection framework for reconstructing keystrokes from over-the-shoulder video footage using four modalities (MediaPipe, HSV skin filtering, motion detection, Canny edge analysis). Results show F1-scores below 19% in staged settings and complete failure in real-world footage, with skin filters producing 57-205 false touch points per frame. The system does not achieve reliable keystroke reconstruction outside calibrated staged settings.
AI.motivation: To monitor human-computer interaction patterns without direct screen recording access, using video intelligence surveillance (VIDINT) on over-the-shoulder footage as an alternative vector for reconstructing typing sequences.
AI.method: Four parallel detection modalities: (1) MediaPipe anatomical hand landmarks, (2) HSV skin color filtering, (3) temporal frame differencing for motion detection, (4) shape-guided Canny edge analysis. Relative touch coordinates mapped to reference screen layout for keystroke reconstruction.
AI.result: Motion-Only F1=18.5%, Edge-Only F1=18.2%, combined multi-modal F1=16.7%, sequence similarity 3.0% on iOS passcode layout. In real-world footage, detector emits median 57 (peak 205) false touch points per frame. System fails outside staged settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed keystroke reconstruction system does not achieve reliable performance outside calibrated staged settings. The staged result does not survive contact with uncontrolled footage.

title: Benchmark AUC Is Not Deployable Reliability: A Cross-Dataset Audit of Off-the-Shelf Features for Surveillance Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Automated "suspicious behavior" flagging is a headline promise of AI surveillance, and the field reports high frame-level ROC-AUC on standard video anomaly detection benchmarks. Those numbers are measured by training and testing on the same camera and scene. We audit what happens when that assumption is dropped. We build an unsupervised normality model from the all-normal training frames of one dataset, using frozen off-the-shelf embeddings (CLIP, DINOv2, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0) and a nearest-neighbour distance, and score the test frames of the same and of other datasets. Across 4 real datasets (UCSD Ped1, UCSD Ped2, CUHK Avenue, ShanghaiTech) and 4 backbones, same-dataset AUC averages 0.704 but cross-dataset AUC averages 0.499, which is chance: a detector calibrated on one scene is no better than a coin flip on another, and in several pairs it is below chance. The strongest backbone makes this worse, not better: DINOv2 has the best same-dataset AUC (up to 0.901 on Ped2) and the largest cross-dataset drop. The collapse is not an artefact of the scoring rule: replacing the nearest-neighbour detector with a PaDiM-style Mahalanobis detector reproduces it almost exactly (cross-dataset gap 0.202 versus 0.208). Even at a favourable operating point the false-alarm rate is on the order of 31,931 per hour. We conclude that the benchmark numbers quoted for surveillance anomaly detection describe a calibrated laboratory setting and overstate deployable reliability by a wide margin, and we release the code that reproduces every number.

AI.tldr: AI surveillance anomaly detection achieves high ROC-AUC within the same camera/scene, but cross-dataset performance drops to near chance (0.499 AUC), showing that current benchmarks overstate real-world reliability.
AI.motivation: To audit whether standard video anomaly detection benchmarks, which train and test on the same camera and scene, reflect deployable reliability in real-world surveillance settings.
AI.method: Built unsupervised normality models using frozen off-the-shelf embeddings (CLIP, DINOv2, ResNet-50, EfficientNet-B0) and nearest-neighbour distance; tested same-dataset vs. cross-dataset (4 real datasets, 4 backbones); validated with PaDiM-style Mahalanobis detector.
AI.result: Same-dataset AUC averages 0.704, cross-dataset AUC drops to 0.499 (chance); DINOv2 showed best same-dataset (0.901 on Ped2) but largest cross-dataset drop; false-alarm rate up to ~31,931 per hour; gap reproduced with alternative detector (0.202 vs 0.208).
AI.conclusion: Current benchmark numbers are calibrated laboratory results that overstate real-world reliability; cross-scene generalization is unsolved and performance is no better than chance outside the training environment.

title: Scenes as Objects, Not Primitives: Instance-Structured 3D Tokenization from Unposed Views
summary: A 3D scene is understood through its objects, not the primitives that compose them. Yet feed-forward reconstruction methods output dense, unstructured sets of points or Gaussians, leaving object-level structure to be recovered after the fact. We propose a feed-forward framework that decomposes a scene into instance-structured 3D token groups directly from unposed multi-view images -- compact object-centric units from which reconstruction, segmentation, and manipulation all follow. Each token group pairs an instance token capturing entity-level identity with anchor tokens that encode local geometry and appearance, which are decoded into a set of 3D Gaussians. This two-level factorization decouples object identity from local appearance, making object instances a native interface of the representation rather than a derived product. The token groups are learned through differentiable rendering with joint reconstruction and segmentation supervision, requiring no 3D annotations. Our feed-forward model surpasses per-scene optimization baselines in class-agnostic instance segmentation while remaining competitive in novel view synthesis. Beyond these metrics, the same token groups directly unlock instance-level scene editing -- removing, translating, or inserting objects by operating on their groups -- as well as efficient open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval, where retrieval complexity scales with the number of instances rather than primitives.

AI.tldr: A feed-forward framework that decomposes 3D scenes into instance-structured token groups from unposed multi-view images, enabling object-centric reconstruction, segmentation, and manipulation without 3D annotations.
AI.motivation: 3D scene understanding should be object-centric rather than primitive-based, but existing feed-forward methods output unstructured points/Gaussians, requiring post-hoc object recovery. The paper aims to make object instances a native interface of the representation.
AI.method: Proposes a two-level factorization: instance tokens (entity-level identity) paired with anchor tokens (local geometry/appearance), decoded into 3D Gaussians. Learned via differentiable rendering with joint reconstruction and segmentation supervision, requiring no 3D annotations.
AI.result: Surpasses per-scene optimization baselines in class-agnostic instance segmentation while remaining competitive in novel view synthesis. Enables instance-level editing (removal, translation, insertion) and efficient open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Token groups serve as a native object-centric representation that unifies reconstruction, segmentation, editing, and retrieval in a single feed-forward framework.

title: MotionAtlas: Detailed Region Captioning for Motion-Centric Videos
summary: We propose MotionAtlas, a system for detailed captioning of motion-centric videos, comprising (1) a dedicated human-annotated benchmark, (2) a scalable, high-quality pipeline to construct training samples, and (3) a family of powerful Video-MLLMs. Unlike conventional global motion captioning datasets, we focus on region-aware motion captioning: given a video and a spatiotemporal mask, the model generates precise descriptions of motion within the target region, thereby alleviating visual clutter and motion entanglement and enabling reliable, quantifiable evaluation. Concretely, we first build MotionAtlas-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 2,073 multiple-choice questions, meticulously annotated for a curated set of high-quality, motion-centric videos, to evaluate fine-grained motion understanding of the objects in question. Second, we design a rigorous and scalable data pipeline that leverages self-bootstrap refinement to suppress fine-grained hallucinations, yielding 159k high-quality motion captioning data. Third, we design a tailored training data composition strategy, which achieves consistent and substantial performance gains across diverse baseline Video-MLLMs, including Molmo2 and Qwen3-VL. For instance, MotionAtlas-4B surpasses Qwen3-VL-4B by an average of 5.2 percentage points across general motion benchmarks. The benchmark, dataset, and code have been released.

AI.tldr: MotionAtlas is a system for detailed, region-aware motion captioning in videos, including a benchmark (MotionAtlas-Bench with 2,073 MCQs), a scalable data pipeline (159k samples via self-bootstrap refinement), and powerful Video-MLLMs that outperform baselines like Qwen3-VL-4B by 5.2% on general motion benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Conventional global motion captioning datasets suffer from visual clutter and motion entanglement; there is a need for region-aware motion captioning that focuses on specific spatiotemporal regions to enable precise, quantifiable evaluation.
AI.method: (1) MotionAtlas-Bench: 2,073 multiple-choice questions for fine-grained motion understanding. (2) A scalable data pipeline using self-bootstrap refinement to suppress hallucinations, producing 159k high-quality samples. (3) A tailored training data composition strategy applied to diverse Video-MLLMs (Molmo2, Qwen3-VL).
AI.result: MotionAtlas-4B surpasses Qwen3-VL-4B by an average of 5.2 percentage points across general motion benchmarks, with consistent improvements across diverse baseline models.
AI.conclusion: Region-aware motion captioning with high-quality data and tailored training strategies significantly improves fine-grained video motion understanding; benchmark, data, and code are publicly released.

title: GarmentZoom: Generating Zoomable Images from Garment Listings
summary: Online product listings for garments often include an overview photo and a close-up to show garment details. However, each photo focuses on either field of view or garment detail, forcing users to alternate between views and breaking browsing continuity. We present GarmentZoom, a system that enhances the full-view photo to match the fidelity of its accompanying close-up, enabling seamless zoom-and-pan exploration. Unlike standard reference-based super-resolution, our setting involves close-up references that are spatially unaligned with the full view, and scale factors that vary substantially across garments 3-20$\times$. Prior work typically relies on alignment to transfer details or requires per-instance fine-tuning to memorize them. Instead, we train a single model that supports a continuous range of scales across diverse garments. Our approach synthesizes details without requiring spatial alignment and matches the quality of per-instance methods with a fraction of the training cost.

AI.tldr: GarmentZoom enhances full-view garment photos using unaligned close-ups as references, enabling seamless zoom-and-pan without per-instance fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Online garment listings show overview and close-up photos separately, forcing users to switch views and breaking browsing continuity.
AI.method: A single model trained on diverse garments that synthesizes details from spatially unaligned close-up references across a continuous 3-20× scale range, without requiring alignment or per-instance fine-tuning.
AI.result: Matches the quality of per-instance fine-tuning methods at a fraction of the training cost.
AI.conclusion: GarmentZoom enables seamless zoom-and-pan exploration of garment photos by enhancing full views with close-up details in a unified, efficient model.

title: Reliability-Prioritized Fine-Grained Generation in Multimodal Large
comment: Equal contribution: Xiaomeng Fan and Wu Wei. Corresponding authors: Zhi Gao and Yunde Jia
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly expected to generate fine-grained descriptions of visual content. However, we observe and theoretically show that generating fine-grained responses poses a reliability challenge, \textit{i.e.}, fine-grained generation is more error-prone than coarse-grained generation. This phenomenon suggests that models should generate the finest description that remains reliable rather than simply produce more specific outputs. To investigate this problem, we develop \textsc{GranFact}, a granularity-aware benchmark consisting of expert-verified multi-object images with coarse-to-fine category annotations. Then, we design a hierarchy-aware evaluation algorithm, which assesses both whether model predictions are visually correct and how specific the correct predictions are. We also propose a reliability-prioritized preference optimization method based on Direct Preference Optimization, which penalizes unreliable fine-grained claims while rewarding reliable specificity. Experiments on \textsc{GranFact} show that our method improves fine-grained generation while preserving reliability. Code and data are available \href{https://github.com/WeiWu2025/GranFact}{here}.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that fine-grained visual descriptions from MLLMs are more error-prone than coarse ones, proposes GranFact benchmark with hierarchy-aware evaluation, and introduces a reliability-prioritized preference optimization method to improve fine-grained generation without sacrificing reliability.
AI.motivation: MLLMs are expected to generate fine-grained visual descriptions, but fine-grained generation is inherently more unreliable and error-prone than coarse-grained generation, posing a reliability challenge.
AI.method: The authors develop GranFact (a granularity-aware benchmark with coarse-to-fine annotations), design a hierarchy-aware evaluation algorithm, and propose a reliability-prioritized preference optimization method based on DPO that penalizes unreliable fine-grained claims while rewarding reliable specificity.
AI.result: Experiments on GranFact show that the proposed method improves fine-grained generation while preserving reliability.
AI.conclusion: Models should generate the finest description that remains reliable rather than simply produce more specific outputs; the proposed optimization method effectively balances specificity and reliability.

title: ReMAP-PET: Beyond Visual Understanding -- Learning Region-Guided Metabolic Alignment Semantics from Brain PET
summary: Positron Emission Tomography (PET) reveals brain metabolism and is clinically central to neurodegenerative disease assessment, yet existing 3D brain foundation models treat PET as generic volumetric data, missing the structured regional metabolic information that distinguishes it from structural neuroimaging. To address these limitations, we propose ReMAP-PET, a framework that moves beyond visual encoding by supervising a partially-tuned MedicalNet 3D ResNet-50 with brain regional standardized uptake value ratio (SUVR) profiles through joint regression and contrastive objectives, enabling the encoder to learn the metabolic semantics underlying PET modality. On 1015 paired PET--SUVR samples, ReMAP-PET achieves 0.070 SUVR MAE and 77.8% PET SUVR Recall@1, substantially outperforming five frozen pretrained baselines. We further connect the metabolic embedding to clinical language via contrastive alignment with frozen BioClinicalBERT and demonstrate end-to-end PET-to-report generation through SUVR-constrained verbalization. Linear probing on diagnostic classification and cognitive regression tasks confirms that the embeddings retain clinically relevant information without task-specific fine-tuning. Our results show that grounding PET encoders in regional metabolic semantics -- rather than treating PET as generic volumetric data -- yields representations that are structured, interpretable, and language-compatible, pointing to a new direction for metabolic-aware PET understanding.

AI.tldr: ReMAP-PET is a framework that learns structured metabolic representations from PET scans by supervising a 3D ResNet-50 with regional SUVR profiles, achieving superior performance on SUVR prediction, retrieval, and downstream clinical tasks while enabling PET-to-report generation.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D brain foundation models treat PET as generic volumetric data, ignoring the structured regional metabolic information that distinguishes it from structural neuroimaging. This limits PET-specific understanding.
AI.method: A partially-tuned MedicalNet 3D ResNet-50 is supervised with brain regional SUVR profiles using joint regression and contrastive objectives. The metabolic embedding is then aligned with frozen BioClinicalBERT via contrastive learning for language compatibility.
AI.result: ReMAP-PET achieves 0.070 SUVR MAE and 77.8% PET SUVR Recall@1 on 1015 paired samples, outperforming five frozen pretrained baselines. Linear probing shows clinically relevant information is retained for diagnostic and cognitive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Grounding PET encoders in regional metabolic semantics yields representations that are structured, interpretable, and language-compatible, pointing to a new direction for metabolic-aware PET understanding.

title: ScAle: Attention Head Scaling as a Minimal Adapter for Spatial Reasoning in Vision Language Models
summary: Spatial reasoning remains a persistent challenge for many vision language models (VLMs), and improving it typically requires fine-tuning with substantial additional parameters. Our preliminary analysis reveals that rescaling activations in selected transformer layers-without modifying pretrained weights-can significantly influence downstream performance. Motivated by this observation, we propose ScAle, an ultra-lightweight adaptation method that learns a small set of scalar coefficients to modulate last-token attention and MLP activations in a fully frozen backbone. We evaluate our method on the synthetic spatial reasoning benchmark SpatialEval and on real-world VQA datasets (COCOQA and VGQA) across multiple model families. Our method, ScAle, achieves up to 134.1% relative accuracy gains using only 1K trainable parameters without requiring millions of trainable parameters as in standard PEFT methods such as LoRA. Despite its extreme compactness, our approach recovers a substantial fraction of standard PEFT performance while preserving strong non-spatial VQA accuracy. These results demonstrate that bounded activation reweighting provides a simple, architecture-agnostic, and highly parameter-efficient alternative for adapting pretrained VLMs.

AI.tldr: ScAle is an ultra-lightweight method that improves spatial reasoning in VLMs by learning scalar coefficients to modulate last-token attention and MLP activations in frozen backbones, achieving up to 134.1% accuracy gains with only 1K trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: Spatial reasoning remains challenging for VLMs, and existing improvements require fine-tuning with many additional parameters. The authors observed that rescaling activations in selected transformer layers can significantly affect performance without modifying pretrained weights.
AI.method: ScAle learns a small set of scalar coefficients to modulate last-token attention and MLP activations in a fully frozen backbone, requiring only 1K trainable parameters.
AI.result: Achieves up to 134.1% relative accuracy gains on spatial reasoning benchmarks using only 1K parameters, recovering substantial PEFT performance while preserving non-spatial VQA accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Bounded activation reweighting offers a simple, architecture-agnostic, and highly parameter-efficient alternative for adapting pretrained VLMs to spatial reasoning tasks.

title: SonoCLIP: Mask-Guided Region-Aware Vision-Language Pretraining for Fetal Ultrasound Analysis
summary: Vision-language foundation models have shown strong potential in medical image analysis. Although foundation models for ultrasound imaging have recently emerged, the domain remains particularly challenging due to severe speckle noise, acquisition variability, and subtle anatomical boundaries, leading to high inter-observer variability. Existing CLIP-based models rely primarily on global image-text alignment, limiting their sensitivity to clinically decisive local structures. We propose SonoCLIP, the first million-scale region-controllable fetal ultrasound vision-language foundation model that integrates segmentation masks as mask-channel visual prompts within the vision encoder, enabling joint global-local contrastive representation learning. To support scalable region-text alignment, we introduce a sigmoid-based pairwise contrastive loss that improves stability under large-scale supervision. We further curate a 1.44M-image multimodal fetal ultrasound dataset spanning 24 standard planes for large-scale pretraining. Extensive cross-center evaluations demonstrate that SonoCLIP achieves superior zero-shot transfer performance under both global and mask-guided inference, establishing a controllable and clinically oriented foundation model for fetal ultrasound analysis. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/Harrison-one/SonoCLIP.

AI.tldr: SonoCLIP is the first million-scale region-controllable fetal ultrasound vision-language foundation model that integrates segmentation masks as visual prompts in CLIP, using a sigmoid-based pairwise contrastive loss for joint global-local representation learning on a newly curated 1.44M-image multimodal dataset, achieving superior zero-shot transfer performance.
AI.motivation: Ultrasound imaging faces challenges like speckle noise, acquisition variability, and subtle anatomical boundaries, causing high inter-observer variability. Existing CLIP-based models rely on global image-text alignment, limiting sensitivity to clinically important local structures.
AI.method: (1) Integrates segmentation masks as mask-channel visual prompts within the vision encoder of CLIP for joint global-local contrastive learning. (2) Introduces a sigmoid-based pairwise contrastive loss for stable large-scale supervision. (3) Curates a 1.44M-image multimodal fetal ultrasound dataset spanning 24 standard planes.
AI.result: Superior zero-shot transfer performance under both global and mask-guided inference across cross-center evaluations.
AI.conclusion: SonoCLIP establishes a controllable and clinically oriented foundation model for fetal ultrasound analysis, advancing region-controllable vision-language pretraining in the ultrasound domain.

title: One Scene, Two Depths: Probing Geometric Ambiguity in Monocular Foundation Models
comment: 49 pages, 25 figures; Accepted by European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) 2026
summary: A faithful 3D world representation should account for layered geometry, where a single camera ray may contain multiple visible and geometrically valid surfaces. Monocular depth estimation, however, reduces this structure to one scalar depth per pixel. Transparent scenes make this ambiguity measurable: the same ray can pass through foreground glass and observe the background, turning the supervised target into a convention of annotation, data, and training rather than a scene-intrinsic truth. A learned predictor exposes this convention as its depth-layer preference. We introduce MultiDepth-3k (MD-3k), a sparse two-layer ordinal benchmark for measuring depth-layer preference and multi-layer spatial relationship accuracy (ML-SRA). On MD-3k, leading depth foundation models exhibit diverse layer preferences under standard RGB input, showing that the same layered geometry can be resolved differently across models. We further find that Laplacian Visual Prompting (LVP), a training-free spectral input transformation, can substantially change the reported layer for certain frozen models. The strongest RGB/LVP pair, DAv2-L, reaches 75.5% ML-SRA. These results suggest that depth foundation models may express complementary geometric hypotheses that standard RGB inference leaves unexpressed. We invite the community to rethink depth supervision and evaluation through an ambiguity-aware lens, where multiple valid 3D interpretations are treated as geometric structure to be measured, preserved, and expressed.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MultiDepth-3k, a benchmark for evaluating how monocular depth models handle layered geometry (e.g., transparent glass with visible background). It shows different models prefer different depth layers, and Laplacian Visual Prompting can shift these preferences. The work argues depth estimation should be treated as an ambiguity-aware problem with multiple valid interpretations rather than a single ground truth.
AI.motivation: Monocular depth estimation traditionally reduces a 3D scene to one scalar depth per pixel, ignoring layered geometry (e.g., transparent surfaces). This creates ambiguity in what the "correct" depth is, leading to inconsistent supervision and evaluation.
AI.method: The authors introduce MultiDepth-3k (MD-3k), a sparse two-layer ordinal benchmark to measure depth-layer preference and multi-layer spatial relationship accuracy (ML-SRA). They evaluate leading depth foundation models under RGB input and apply Laplacian Visual Prompting (LVP), a training-free spectral transformation, to shift layer preferences.
AI.result: Different depth models exhibit diverse layer preferences on MD-3k, showing the same geometry is resolved differently. LVP substantially changes reported layers for some frozen models. The best RGB/LVP pair (DAv2-L) achieves 75.5% ML-SRA.
AI.conclusion: Depth foundation models express complementary geometric hypotheses not visible under standard RGB inference. The community should rethink depth supervision/evaluation as ambiguity-aware, treating multiple valid 3D interpretations as structure to be measured and preserved.

title: Benchmarking Geospatial Foundation Models for Agriculture Applications
summary: Geospatial foundation models pretrained on satellite imagery promise broad generalization across remote sensing tasks and regions, but their geographic transferability has not been systematically tested, especially in agriculture applications. This paper presents a controlled benchmark that evaluates three models, Prithvi, SpectralGPT, and SatMAE, on multi-temporal crop segmentation and change detection across four U.S. states, Iowa, North Carolina, California, and Minnesota. By assigning each train, validation, and test split to a separate region, we measure how well each model transfers to land it has not seen. All three degrade sharply under regional distribution shift, predicting only the most common crops while missing rare ones. We further find that fitting these models to a shared input format affects each one differently, which complicates direct architectural comparison. These results expose key limitations of current geospatial foundation models for agriculture and point to region aware evaluation as a necessary standard.

AI.tldr: Geospatial foundation models (Prithvi, SpectralGPT, SatMAE) degrade sharply in agricultural tasks when tested on unseen regions, predicting only common crops and missing rare ones.
AI.motivation: The geographic transferability of geospatial foundation models has not been systematically tested, especially in agriculture applications.
AI.method: A controlled benchmark evaluating three models (Prithvi, SpectralGPT, SatMAE) on multi-temporal crop segmentation and change detection across four U.S. states, with train/validation/test splits assigned to separate regions.
AI.result: All three models degrade sharply under regional distribution shift, predicting only the most common crops while missing rare ones. Fitting models to a shared input format affects each model differently, complicating direct architectural comparison.
AI.conclusion: Current geospatial foundation models have key limitations for agriculture, and region-aware evaluation should become a necessary standard.

title: Unlocking the Visual Record of Materials Science: A Large-Scale Multimodal Dataset from Scientific Literature
summary: The materials science literature encodes decades of experimental knowledge in figures, yet this visual record remains locked away and inaccessible to AI at scale. The core difficulty is structural: most scientific figures are compound, with a single caption describing multiple sub-panels simultaneously, making direct image-text pairing unreliable. We present MatMMExtract, an end-to-end open-source pipeline that resolves this by decomposing compound figures into individual sub-panels and generating structured, grounded annotations using a large language model guided by a curated materials science taxonomy. Applied to 14,810 open-access articles, MatMMExtract produces MatSciFig; 391,606 panel-level image-text pairs from 180,571 figures, each annotated with a sub-caption, a two-level visualisation category spanning 19 classes and over 100 subtypes, and a scientific summary. To enable accurate panel localisation, we introduce MaterialScope, a domain-specific detection dataset of 2,811 manually annotated materials science figures, on which a fine-tuned YOLO12-m detector achieves mAP_50 of 0.9227. Among six benchmarked language models, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite delivers the best cost-quality trade-off for annotation generation, with 82% of outputs rated good and a hallucination rate of 4.8%. A dual-encoder retrieval baseline on MatSciFig achieves a 4.4 times improvement in R@1 over zero-shot CLIP, demonstrating the dataset's immediate utility for vision-language learning. All resources are released openly to the community.

AI.tldr: MatMMExtract is an open-source pipeline that decomposes compound scientific figures into sub-panels with structured annotations, producing MatSciFig—a dataset of 391,606 panel-level image-text pairs from materials science literature.
AI.motivation: Materials science figures contain rich experimental knowledge, but most are compound figures (multiple sub-panels under one caption), making direct image-text pairing unreliable and inaccessible to AI at scale.
AI.method: MatMMExtract decomposes compound figures using a fine-tuned YOLO12-m detector (MaterialScope dataset), then generates grounded annotations via LLM (Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite) guided by a materials science taxonomy.
AI.result: 391,606 panel-level pairs from 14,810 articles; detector achieves mAP_50 of 0.9227; 82% of outputs rated good with 4.8% hallucination rate; dual-encoder retrieval achieves 4.4× improvement in R@1 over CLIP.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline effectively unlocks visual knowledge from materials science literature at scale, and the released dataset demonstrates immediate utility for vision-language learning tasks.

title: PoseShield: Neural Collision Fields for Human Self-Collision Resolution
summary: Self-collision remains a persistent challenge in SMPL-based human pose estimation and motion generation. Under extreme articulations or stochastic motion synthesis, generated meshes frequently exhibit self-penetrations, leading to physically implausible results. We propose PoseShield, a neural collision constraint defined directly in SMPL pose space. We formulate collision correction as a constrained optimization problem and connect the learned constraint with the Eikonal equation. Enforcing Eikonal regularization ensures non-vanishing gradients near the collision boundary, improving numerical stability and robustness of the optimization process. Unlike prior methods that operate in the mesh space or rely on heuristic penalties, our approach operates directly in the low-dimensional space of human poses and is theoretically grounded. The same learned constraint extends to human motion sequences, providing a generator-agnostic post-hoc collision corrector without retraining the underlying motion model. Experiments on a newly constructed SMPL pose benchmark show that our method achieves a 95.8% success rate and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: PoseShield is a neural collision constraint operating directly in SMPL pose space that corrects self-collisions in human poses and motion sequences using Eikonal-regularized constrained optimization, achieving 95.8% success rate.
AI.motivation: Self-collision (self-penetration) in SMPL-based human pose estimation and motion generation remains a persistent challenge, causing physically implausible results under extreme articulations or stochastic motion synthesis.
AI.method: Formulates collision correction as a constrained optimization problem directly in SMPL pose space, connects the learned constraint with the Eikonal equation, and enforces Eikonal regularization for non-vanishing gradients near collision boundaries.
AI.result: Achieves 95.8% success rate on a newly constructed SMPL pose benchmark, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: The same learned constraint generalizes to human motion sequences as a generator-agnostic post-hoc collision corrector without retraining, offering a theoretically grounded and numerically stable approach compared to prior mesh-space or heuristic methods.

title: Progressive Self-Supervised Learning with Individualized Community Assignment for Brain Network Analysis
summary: Brain networks exhibit a modular community structure that varies across individuals and neurological conditions. However, existing self-supervised learning (SSL) methods often overlook this heterogeneity, relying on generic masking strategies that fail to capture subject-specific functional organization. We propose BrainPICM, a self-supervised framework for brain network analysis via progressive individualized community aware masking. BrainPICM formulates ROI-to-community mapping as a progressive unbalanced optimal transport process, yielding soft assignments and per-ROI confidence scores. Guided by these confidence estimates, a curriculum-style masking strategy gradually incorporates low-confidence, potentially pathological regions into training, enabling the model to learn both stable modular structures and individual variations. Additionally, a deviation-aware aggregation module quantifies functional reorganization by measuring mass redistribution relative to a population template, enhancing interpretability and downstream prediction. Experiments on three fMRI datasets (ABIDE-I, ADHD-200, ADNI) show that BrainPICM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and SSL methods in diagnostic accuracy, indicating that explicitly injecting modular community structure into masked modeling yields more functionally consistent and generalizable representations. The source code for this approach will be released at https://github.com/Hrychen7/BrainPICM.

AI.tldr: BrainPICM is a self-supervised framework for brain network analysis that uses progressive individualized community-aware masking via unbalanced optimal transport to capture both stable modular structures and subject-specific variations, outperforming existing methods on three fMRI datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing SSL methods for brain network analysis rely on generic masking strategies that overlook the heterogeneity of modular community structure across individuals and neurological conditions, failing to capture subject-specific functional organization.
AI.method: (1) Progressive individualized community-aware masking via unbalanced optimal transport for soft ROI-to-community mapping with confidence scores. (2) Curriculum-style masking that gradually incorporates low-confidence (potentially pathological) regions. (3) Deviation-aware aggregation module measuring mass redistribution relative to a population template.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and SSL methods on three fMRI datasets (ABIDE-I, ADHD-200, ADNI) in diagnostic accuracy, producing more functionally consistent and generalizable representations.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly injecting modular community structure into masked modeling yields better representations, and the approach enables interpretable quantification of functional reorganization.

title: MF-UAVPose6D: A Model-Free Monocular 6-DoF Pose Estimation Framework for Fixed-Wing UAVs
summary: For uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs), estimating six-degree-of-freedom (6-DoF) poses is essential for airspace situational awareness, target tracking, and counter-UAV operations. However, non-cooperative targets usually lack computer-aided design (CAD) models and keypoint priors, making existing model-based or keypoint-matching methods difficult to apply reliably. To address these challenges, this paper proposes MF-UAVPose6D, a model-free monocular 6-DoF pose estimation framework for fixed-wing UAVs. During inference, the method takes only a single red-green-blue (RGB) image and camera intrinsics as input. It first obtains a stable target anchor through heatmap-guided center localization, introduces a Perspective-Aware Module (PAM) to model observation-ray priors, exploits Dynamic Topological Sampling (DTS) to complement weak structural cues from the wings, fuselage, and tail, and adopts a decoupled translation-rotation pose decoding mechanism to estimate the 6-DoF pose. In addition, we construct the FW-UAV6DPose synthetic dataset, which covers fixed-wing UAV observations across diverse distances, viewpoints, and poses. Experimental results show that MF-UAVPose6D achieves accurate and efficient monocular 6-DoF pose estimation without requiring CAD models, and demonstrates strong robustness in long-range rotation estimation, depth recovery, and joint pose evaluation.

AI.tldr: MF-UAVPose6D is a model-free monocular 6-DoF pose estimation framework for fixed-wing UAVs that uses only a single RGB image and camera intrinsics, employing heatmap-guided center localization, a Perspective-Aware Module, Dynamic Topological Sampling, and decoupled translation-rotation decoding to achieve accurate pose estimation without CAD models or keypoint priors.
AI.motivation: Estimating 6-DoF poses of non-cooperative UAVs is essential for airspace situational awareness and counter-UAV operations, but existing methods rely on CAD models or keypoint priors that are unavailable for unknown targets, creating a need for a model-free approach.
AI.method: The framework uses heatmap-guided center localization for stable target anchoring, a Perspective-Aware Module (PAM) to model observation-ray priors, Dynamic Topological Sampling (DTS) to extract weak structural cues from wings/fuselage/tail, and a decoupled translation-rotation pose decoding mechanism. The FW-UAV6DPose synthetic dataset was also constructed.
AI.result: The method achieves accurate and efficient monocular 6-DoF pose estimation without CAD models, showing strong robustness in long-range rotation estimation, depth recovery, and joint pose evaluation.
