summary: Table parsing aims to convert table images into structured, machine-readable representations, a task requiring the joint perception of complex spatial layouts and textual content. While recent vision-language models (VLMs) enable end-to-end parsing, they typically rely on direct supervision of the final output, thereby bypassing the explicit intermediate reasoning that is crucial for understanding complex table structures. Furthermore, attempts to optimize these models using reinforcement learning (RL) are often hindered by unstable or ambiguous reward designs, limiting potential performance gains. To address these limitations, we propose StrucTab, a table parsing model learned through intermediate structural supervision and reward decomposition. At the modeling level, by decomposing the parsing process into human-inspired subtasks, such as row-column counting and merged-cell analysis, StrucTab progressively unifies them through a sequential reasoning strategy. At the optimization level, we introduce Uni-TabRL, a unified RL framework that leverages decomposed rewards (validity, structure, and content) to provide stable and informative optimization signals. Finally, at the evaluation level, we present TableVerse-5K, a large-scale, challenging benchmark encompassing diverse, real-world table scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of StrucTab across all evaluated public benchmarks and significant improvements on TableVerse-5K, validating the effectiveness of explicit structural modeling and decomposed reward optimization. Code and benchmark are publicly available at https://github.com/VirtualLUOUCAS/StrucTab.

AI.tldr: StrucTab is a table parsing model that uses intermediate structural supervision and a decomposed reward RL framework (Uni-TabRL) to achieve state-of-the-art performance, validated on public benchmarks and a new large-scale dataset TableVerse-5K.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-based table parsing methods rely on direct end-to-end supervision, bypassing explicit intermediate reasoning. Additionally, RL optimization is hindered by unstable or ambiguous reward designs, limiting performance gains.
AI.method: (1) Decomposes parsing into human-inspired subtasks (row-column counting, merged-cell analysis) with sequential reasoning. (2) Introduces Uni-TabRL, a unified RL framework with decomposed rewards: validity, structure, and content. (3) Proposes TableVerse-5K benchmark.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on all evaluated public benchmarks and significant improvements on the new TableVerse-5K benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Explicit structural modeling via subtask decomposition and decomposed reward optimization effectively improves table parsing performance and training stability.

title: Traffic-CBM: A Structurally Interpretable Multimodal Framework for Encrypted Traffic Classification
summary: Encrypted traffic classification has achieved strong performance, but its decision process remains difficult to interpret. Existing methods usually combine flow statistics, packet sequences, and byte-level representations into opaque latent features, making it unclear which type of evidence actually drives the prediction. In this paper, we propose Traffic-CBM, a structurally interpretable multimodal framework for encrypted traffic classification. Instead of directly fusing heterogeneous traffic signals into a black-box representation, Traffic-CBM organizes them into a unified hierarchical concept space. These concepts are not manually annotated semantic attributes; rather, they are scalar evidence summaries constrained by predefined traffic evidence groups. More specifically, grouped flow statistics are mapped to statistical concepts, dedicated temporal encoders learn temporal concepts from disjoint feature subspaces, and byte-level evidence is further organized into packet-level and cross-packet concepts. This design turns heterogeneous traffic evidence into an explicit concept representation and makes different levels of traffic evidence easier to analyze. We evaluate Traffic-CBM on multiple encrypted traffic benchmarks. Results show that it achieves competitive and balanced classification performance while providing a clearer structural interpretation interface than conventional end-to-end fusion models. Further analyses suggest that the learned concept space is actively used in the prediction process and provides a clearer structural explanation of multimodal traffic evidence.

AI.tldr: Traffic-CBM is a structurally interpretable multimodal framework for encrypted traffic classification that organizes heterogeneous traffic signals into a unified hierarchical concept space instead of opaque latent features, achieving competitive performance while providing clear structural explanations.
AI.motivation: Existing encrypted traffic classification methods achieve strong performance but produce opaque decisions by fusing flow statistics, packet sequences, and byte-level representations into black-box latent features, making it unclear which evidence drives predictions.
AI.method: Traffic-CBM organizes heterogeneous traffic signals into a unified hierarchical concept space with predefined traffic evidence groups: grouped flow statistics map to statistical concepts, temporal encoders learn temporal concepts from disjoint feature subspaces, and byte-level evidence is organized into packet-level and cross-packet concepts, all without requiring manual semantic annotations.
AI.result: Evaluated on multiple encrypted traffic benchmarks, Traffic-CBM achieves competitive and balanced classification performance while providing a clearer structural interpretation interface than conventional end-to-end fusion models.
AI.conclusion: The learned concept space is actively used in the prediction process and provides a clearer structural explanation of multimodal traffic evidence, outperforming black-box fusion models in interpretability without sacrificing classification performance.

title: H-GRPO: Permutation-Invariant Reinforcement Learning for Grounded Visual Reasoning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often achieve high performance on benchmarks while remaining "black boxes", yet they remain prone to hallucination or rely on superficial shortcuts. In this work, we propose a framework designed to enhance both performance and interpretability through De-compositional Evidence Grounding. Unlike monolithic inference approaches, our approach forces the model to decompose a global query into a sequence of atomic sub-questions, each requiring an explicit sub-answer and critically a localized evidence bounding box. By grounding intermediate logical steps (e.g. identifying a container, analyzing liquid properties, and assessing environmental context) in specific visual regions, we construct a structured reasoning path that mirrors human-like deduction. This allows the final answer to emerge as a logical consequence of verified visual facts rather than a statistical guess.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Decompositional Evidence Grounding framework that forces VLMs to break down queries into atomic sub-questions with evidence bounding boxes, creating interpretable reasoning paths for more reliable and transparent answers.
AI.motivation: VLMs are black boxes prone to hallucinations and superficial shortcuts despite high benchmark performance, lacking interpretability and reliable reasoning.
AI.method: Decompositional Evidence Grounding: decomposes global queries into atomic sub-questions, each requiring explicit sub-answers and localized evidence bounding boxes, constructing structured reasoning paths.
AI.result: The framework produces answers that are logical consequences of verified visual facts rather than statistical guesses, with explicit grounding in specific visual regions.
AI.conclusion: By forcing step-by-step visual evidence grounding, the approach enhances both model performance and interpretability, mimicking human-like deductive reasoning.

title: DCGrasp: Distance-aware Controllable Grasp Generation
summary: Generating 3D hand-object interactions is essential for applications in robotics, XR, and synthetic data generation, where flexible controllability and strong generalization to diverse object geometries are required. However, existing methods rarely satisfy these requirements, limiting their practical applicability. We present DCGrasp, a distance-aware controllable grasp generation system built on a novel grasp energy term. This term computes Distance Profile, a signed distance from each hand vertex to the nearest object point, coupled with distance-aware weighting, effectively capturing the semantically similar hand-object interaction in near-contact regions while remaining invariant to object and hand identity. Given various controllable signals, DCGrasp first generates a Distance Profile based on a Diffusion Transformer, together with a corresponding candidate hand pose. We then refine the candidate pose through optimization, enforcing consistency between the optimized hand pose and the generated Distance Profile in near-contact regions. Our experiments show that DCGrasp produces high-quality, physically plausible grasps with flexible user control, generalizing to diverse object and hand shapes and scales. Our work establishes a robust and versatile pipeline for the synthesis of controllable 3D hand-object interactions.

AI.tldr: DCGrasp is a distance-aware controllable grasp generation system that uses a novel Distance Profile (signed distances from hand vertices to nearest object points with distance-aware weighting) to generate high-quality, physically plausible 3D hand-object grasps with flexible user control, generalizing well across diverse object and hand shapes.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for generating 3D hand-object interactions rarely satisfy the requirements of flexible controllability and strong generalization to diverse object geometries, limiting their practical applicability in robotics, XR, and synthetic data generation.
AI.method: DCGrasp introduces a Distance Profile (signed distance from each hand vertex to nearest object point with distance-aware weighting) that captures semantically similar hand-object interaction in near-contact regions while remaining invariant to object and hand identity. It first generates a Distance Profile via a Diffusion Transformer along with a candidate hand pose, then refines the pose through optimization to enforce consistency between the optimized pose and generated Distance Profile in near-contact regions.
AI.result: DCGrasp produces high-quality, physically plausible grasps with flexible user control, demonstrating strong generalization to diverse object and hand shapes and scales.
AI.conclusion: DCGrasp establishes a robust and versatile pipeline for the synthesis of controllable 3D hand-object interactions, satisfying requirements for flexible controllability and strong generalization.

title: Latent-CURE for Breast Cancer Diagnosis
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Accepted to MICCAI 2026
summary: Multimodal Large Models have significantly advanced automated breast ultrasound diagnosis. However, most existing frameworks utilize opaque, end-to-end paradigms prioritizing global statistical correlations over structured clinical reasoning. Consequently, these models remain susceptible to shortcut learning amid extreme real-world epidemiological imbalances, often bypassing rare but decisive malignant indicators for dominant benign patterns. To address this disconnect, we propose Latent-CURE, a novel diagnostic framework driven by asymmetric weighted chain-of-thought methodology grounded in latent space reasoning. Unlike traditional approaches, our framework constructs an implicit reasoning trajectory forcing the model to sequentially infer standardized BI-RADS morphological descriptors before converging on a final diagnosis. Furthermore, to combat the extreme scarcity of critical malignant features, we couple this architecture with a dual-asymmetric optimization strategy. By dynamically adjusting margins and weights, this strategy safeguards high-specificity malignant descriptors from being overshadowed by common benign priors. Comprehensive evaluations demonstrate that our knowledge-injected approach provides transparent clinical evidence while achieving robust, accurate diagnostic performance in imbalanced medical cohorts.

AI.tldr: Latent-CURE is a diagnostic framework for breast ultrasound that uses asymmetric weighted chain-of-thought reasoning in latent space to infer BI-RADS morphological descriptors before diagnosis, coupled with a dual-asymmetric optimization strategy to handle extreme class imbalance, achieving robust and transparent performance.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal large models for breast ultrasound diagnosis use opaque end-to-end paradigms that rely on global statistical correlations, making them susceptible to shortcut learning. They often ignore rare but decisive malignant indicators in favor of dominant benign patterns due to extreme epidemiological imbalances.
AI.method: (1) Asymmetric weighted chain-of-thought (CoT) methodology in latent space: constructs an implicit reasoning trajectory that forces the model to sequentially infer standardized BI-RADS morphological descriptors before arriving at a final diagnosis. (2) Dual-asymmetric optimization strategy: dynamically adjusts margins and weights to safeguard high-specificity malignant descriptors from being overshadowed by common benign priors.
AI.result: The knowledge-injected approach provides transparent clinical evidence while achieving robust and accurate diagnostic performance in imbalanced medical cohorts.
AI.conclusion: Latent-CURE effectively addresses shortcut learning and class imbalance issues in automated breast ultrasound diagnosis by enforcing structured clinical reasoning (BI-RADS descriptors) and asymmetric optimization, producing both transparent evidence and strong performance.

title: Scene-aware Prediction of Diverse Human Movement Goals
summary: Anticipation of human behaviours facilitates autonomous systems in proactive planning. Human behaviour could be stochastic due to varying goals. Human goals typically guide their own movement and could therefore help to predict the human trajectory and human motion in the long-term. To infer the human movement intentions, the environmental context plays a significant role, in addition to the social cues expressed by the individual. Previous works on human goals prediction either require semantic knowledge of the scene, or only tackle interactions with objects. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-goal prediction method using the generative model to address the stochasticity of human movement. It leverages the current RGB scene and the human pose to predict diverse potential future goals of human movement based on the Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE). Our results demonstrate that our approach is capable of generating multiple movement goals in the scene via samplings in latent space of the CVAE and exhibits generalization capability across scenarios in GTA-IM dataset and PROX dataset. Code is publicly available at \href{https://github.com/Q-Y-Yang/DiverseGoalsPrediction.git}{\texttt{https://github.com/Q-Y-Yang/DiverseGoalsPrediction}}.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a CVAE-based multi-goal prediction method that uses RGB scene images and human pose to generate diverse potential future goals of human movement, addressing stochasticity in human behavior.
AI.motivation: Human behavior is stochastic with varying goals; predicting diverse future goals enables autonomous systems to proactively plan, but previous works require semantic scene knowledge or only handle object interactions.
AI.method: Uses Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) to model multi-goal prediction by leveraging current RGB scene and human pose, sampling in latent space to generate diverse potential movement goals.
AI.result: The approach generates multiple movement goals via latent space sampling and shows generalization across GTA-IM and PROX datasets.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively captures stochasticity in human goal prediction without requiring semantic scene knowledge, and generalizes well across different scenarios.

title: Explainability-Aware Frustum Attack: Exposing Structural Vulnerabilities in LiDAR-Based 3D Object Detectors
comment: The 19th European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV 2026)
summary: The structural vulnerabilities of point cloud-based 3D object detectors remain poorly understood. Prior work has studied adversarial robustness primarily on isolated 3D object models, while recent LiDAR spoofing attacks target richer and more realistic driving scenes but focus mainly on physical realizability rather than understanding detector behavior or attack efficiency. In this work, we investigate how LiDAR-based detectors rely on spatial evidence in complex scenes and whether these reliance patterns can be exploited to induce failures more efficiently. To this end, we propose an explainability-guided adversarial analysis methodology. We introduce the Saliency-LiDAR (SALL) method, which aggregates Integrated Gradient attributions across scenes to produce universal saliency maps for LiDAR-based 3D object detectors. Guided by these maps, we design the Explainability-aware Frustum Attack (EFA), which selectively perturbs only the most influential frustums rather than uniformly attacking entire object regions. Experiments on KITTI and nuScenes, across detectors such as PointPillars and SECOND, show that EFA reduces detection recall by more than 15 percentage points while requiring 25-50% fewer perturbed frustums than the state-of-the-art non-saliency-aware baseline. These findings reveal that modern 3D detectors concentrate discriminative evidence in a small subset of spatial regions, exposing a structural robustness vulnerability in current LiDAR perception systems. Our code is released at https://github.com/SecMindLab/Saliency_LiDAR.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Saliency-LiDAR (SALL), an explainability-guided method that creates universal saliency maps to identify spatially critical regions for LiDAR-based 3D detectors. Using these maps, the Explainability-aware Frustum Attack (EFA) selectively perturbs only the most influential frustums, achieving over 15 percentage points recall reduction with 25-50% fewer perturbed frustums than baselines, revealing that 3D detectors rely on small, exploitable spatial regions.
AI.motivation: To understand how LiDAR-based 3D object detectors rely on spatial evidence in complex scenes and whether these reliance patterns can be exploited to induce failures more efficiently than existing attack methods.
AI.method: The authors propose Saliency-LiDAR (SALL), which aggregates Integrated Gradient attributions across scenes to produce universal saliency maps for LiDAR-based 3D detectors. Based on these maps, they design the Explainability-aware Frustum Attack (EFA) that selectively perturbs only the most influential frustums rather than uniformly attacking entire object regions.
AI.result: On KITTI and nuScenes datasets, with detectors like PointPillars and SECOND, EFA reduces detection recall by more than 15 percentage points while requiring 25-50% fewer perturbed frustums compared to state-of-the-art non-saliency-aware baselines.
AI.conclusion: Modern 3D detectors concentrate discriminative evidence in a small subset of spatial regions, revealing a structural robustness vulnerability in current LiDAR perception systems that can be exploited efficiently through saliency-guided attacks.

title: Variance Reduction on the Camera Axis: Multi-View Score Distillation for 3D
comment: 30 pages, 19 figures. Submitted to WACV 2027 (Algorithms Track)
summary: Score distillation turns a pretrained 2D diffusion model into a 3D generator, but the per-step gradient is estimated from a single randomly chosen view: it is high-variance and blind to global shape consistency. Prior work addresses this by retraining the diffusion prior on multi-view data; this improves consistency but makes the sampling contribution inseparable from prior quality. We instead isolate the sampling axis. The per-step gradient is one noisy sample of an expectation over views; aggregating K samples per step at a fixed total UNet budget reduces variance without touching the prior. We introduce Multi-View Aggregated Score Distillation (MV-SDI), which aggregates gradients from K views per step via gradient accumulation, keeping peak memory unchanged and the 2D prior frozen, and draws views as antithetic antipodal pairs, a prior-independent geometric property, for balanced angular coverage. At a fixed 10,000-UNet-call budget, K=2 raises CLIP R-Precision from 74.8% to 83.8% and CLIP score from 0.297 to 0.312, with consistent gains on HPSv2 and ImageReward and a 0.0% divergence rate on the 43-prompt benchmark; optimization steps halve as a consequence. K=4 gives a fourfold step reduction at R-Precision 86.9% and CLIP 0.307, still well above the single-view baseline on every alignment metric. MV-SDI is compatible with gradient-based score-distillation pipelines, including Score Distillation via Inversion, and requires no retraining and no multi-view data.

AI.tldr: MV-SDI improves 3D generation from 2D diffusion priors by aggregating gradients from multiple views per optimization step (via antithetic antipodal sampling), reducing variance and improving 3D consistency without retraining or multi-view data.
AI.motivation: Score distillation suffers from high-variance, view-inconsistent gradient estimates because each step uses only a single randomly chosen view. Prior multi-view solutions retrain the diffusion prior, making gains inseparable from prior quality.
AI.method: MV-SDI aggregates gradients from K views per optimization step via gradient accumulation (keeping peak memory unchanged), draws views as antithetic antipodal pairs for balanced angular coverage, and keeps the 2D prior frozen.
AI.result: At fixed 10K UNet budget: K=2 improves CLIP R-Precision from 74.8% to 83.8%, CLIP score 0.297→0.312, with 0.0% divergence rate; K=4 gives fourfold step reduction at R-Precision 86.9%.
AI.conclusion: MV-SDI is a drop-in, retraining-free enhancement for gradient-based score distillation pipelines that improves quality and consistency by reducing gradient variance through multi-view aggregation.

title: Learning Efficient 4D Gaussian Representations from Monocular Videos with Flow Splatting
summary: Reconstructing dynamic 3D scenes from monocular videos is challenging due to scene complexity and temporal dynamics. With the advancement of 3D Gaussian Splatting in novel view synthesis, existing methods extend 3D Gaussians to 4D domain with deformation fields, trajectories or spatiotemporal 4D volumes to model scene element deformation. However, these methods suffer from long training time, low rendering speed or high memory consumption for per-frame reconstruction of 4D volumes, without fully exploiting dense dynamic information. To address this issue, we propose Flow Splatting, which constructs the velocity field and enables the conventional splatting technique to render optical flow from the velocity field to supervise dynamics learning process from monocular videos. Specifically, we extend 4D volumes with time varying means and covariance to represent complex dynamics. Then, we construct and approximate the velocity field naturally based on this representations. While conventional volume rendering techniques support to render color fields, we extend the volume rendering strategy to splat the velocity field by considering the influence of camera motions. We conduct experiments on various benchmarks to demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of our method. Compared to the state-of-the-art methods, our model achieves better image quality with less time consumption and higher rendering speed.

AI.tldr: Flow Splatting extends 3D Gaussian Splatting to 4D by introducing a velocity field and splatting-based optical flow rendering to efficiently reconstruct dynamic 3D scenes from monocular videos, achieving better quality, faster training, and higher rendering speed than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Existing 4D Gaussian methods suffer from long training times, low rendering speeds, or high memory consumption when reconstructing dynamic scenes from monocular videos, as they fail to fully exploit dense dynamic information.
AI.method: Flow Splatting constructs a velocity field from time-varying 4D Gaussians (with dynamic means and covariances), then extends conventional splatting to render optical flow from this velocity field while accounting for camera motion, enabling direct supervision of dynamics learning.
AI.result: The method achieves better image quality with less training time and higher rendering speed compared to state-of-the-art methods on various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By effectively modeling velocity fields and rendering optical flow via splatting, the proposed approach offers a more efficient and effective solution for dynamic 3D scene reconstruction from monocular videos.

title: Rigel: Self-Distilled Score Adaptation for Image and Video Captioning Evaluation
summary: Automatic evaluation of image and video captioning is essential for benchmarking multimodal systems, although standard evaluation metrics show limited alignment with human judgments. Recent approaches using large language models (LLMs), commonly referred to as LLM-as-a-Judge, have improved alignment with human judgments but still suffer from a mismatch between large-vocabulary language modeling and evaluation over a small label set. To address this, we propose Rigel, an automatic evaluation metric for image and video captioning, based on self-distilled score adaptation. The metric employs an evaluation-specific scoring head distilled from a frozen LLM, which captures judgment signals in a task-aligned space without relying on large-vocabulary token sets. We then refine the LLM backbone with human judgment data. To train Rigel, we constructed the Vid-Lepus dataset, which contains 3,338 video clips, 33,380 reference captions, and 5,637 candidate captions. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that Rigel outperforms state-of-the-art metrics, achieving over 10-point improvements on ActivityNet-Fact in the reference-free setting.

AI.tldr: Rigel is a new automatic evaluation metric for image/video captioning that uses a self-distilled scoring head from a frozen LLM and a new Vid-Lepus dataset, outperforming SOTA metrics by over 10 points on ActivityNet-Fact.
AI.motivation: Existing automatic evaluation metrics for image/video captioning show limited alignment with human judgments. LLM-as-a-Judge methods improve alignment but suffer from a mismatch between large-vocabulary language modeling and evaluation over a small label set.
AI.method: Rigel uses an evaluation-specific scoring head distilled from a frozen LLM, capturing judgment signals in a task-aligned space without relying on large-vocabulary token sets. The LLM backbone is then refined with human judgment data. The Vid-Lepus dataset (3,338 video clips, 33,380 reference captions, 5,637 candidate captions) was constructed for training.
AI.result: Rigel outperforms state-of-the-art metrics across multiple benchmarks, achieving over 10-point improvements on ActivityNet-Fact in the reference-free setting.
AI.conclusion: The self-distilled score adaptation approach effectively bridges the gap between LLM language modeling and evaluation tasks, yielding superior caption evaluation metrics.

title: SICAGE: Speaker-Independent Culture-Aware Gesture Generation using TED4C-L Dataset
summary: Recent co-speech gesture generation methods often overlook cultural differences, limiting their effectiveness in human-agent interaction. Moreover, culture-conditioned models are rarely evaluated under speaker-disjoint splits, so apparent "cultural" behavior may be confounded with speaker-specific gesturing style. We introduce SICAGE, a modular framework for culture-aware co-speech gesture generation that conditions motion synthesis models on speaker-independent cultural representations. SICAGE learns these representations from audio and text by treating each speaker as a separate domain while imposing invariance across speakers. This encourages representations to remain culture-discriminative while reducing dependence on speaker identity. The resulting cultural embeddings condition a multimodal generator to produce culturally appropriate gestures. We instantiate this idea with two domain generalization approaches: adversarial learning and Fishr regularization. We further introduce ALaDiT, a real-time diffusion-based gesture generator designed to efficiently incorporate the learned cultural embeddings. To validate our method, we built TED4C-L, a 106-hour multimodal dataset of 764 TED speakers from four cultural groups. Experiments show that SICAGE improves motion realism, diversity, beat synchronization, semantic relevance, and cultural consistency.

AI.tldr: SICAGE is a modular framework for culture-aware co-speech gesture generation that learns speaker-independent cultural representations from audio and text using domain generalization, achieving improved motion realism, diversity, beat synchronization, semantic relevance, and cultural consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing co-speech gesture generation methods neglect cultural differences and are evaluated without speaker-disjoint splits, causing apparent "cultural" behavior to be confounded with speaker-specific gesturing style.
AI.method: SICAGE learns culture-discriminative, speaker-independent representations via adversarial learning or Fishr regularization (domain generalization), then conditions a multimodal gesture generator (ALaDiT, a real-time diffusion-based model) on these embeddings. Evaluated on the TED4C-L dataset (106h, 764 speakers, 4 cultural groups).
AI.result: SICAGE improves motion realism, diversity, beat synchronization, semantic relevance, and cultural consistency compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling cultural representations from speaker identity is effective for producing culturally appropriate gestures, and the proposed framework generalizes well across unseen speakers.

title: GeoEdit: Geometry-Aware Object Editing via Dual-Branch Denoising
summary: Precisely manipulating objects in a single photograph (translation, rotation, scaling) while obeying 3D physical constraints remains unsolved for diffusion-based editors. Current 2D methods lack spatial awareness and produce perspective violations. Forcing structural proxies into the latent space also disrupts variance homogeneity, and the resulting self-attention leakage leads to ghosting and background blur. The core difficulty is asymmetric: the relocated object must follow a rigid geometry, yet the uncovered background needs freedom to synthesize plausible content. We present GeoEdit, a training-free Lift-Manipulate-Render-Denoise pipeline that satisfies both constraints. We decouple scene and object in 3D, align them through point correspondence, and render a geometry-aligned proxy with a structural depth map. A Dual-Branch Denoising stage then refines this proxy: a video diffusion backbone preserves object identity, while 3D constraints are injected into the foreground within a narrow denoising window at matching noise variance (variance-homogeneous injection). The background denoises freely. Because the injected signal matches the native latent statistics, self-attention stays undisturbed. We also introduce GeoEditBench, a pose-aware benchmark covering object translation, object rotation, and camera movement with pose-aware evaluation metrics. Experiments confirm consistent gains in geometric accuracy, identity fidelity, and background quality. Our codes are available at https://github.com/Heey731/GeoEdit.

AI.tldr: GeoEdit is a training-free pipeline for 3D-constrained object editing in single images, using Lift-Manipulate-Render-Denoise with variance-homogeneous injection to preserve both object identity and background quality without self-attention leakage.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based editors struggle with 3D physical constraints when manipulating objects in single photographs — they produce perspective violations, ghosting, and background blur due to self-attention leakage from forcing structural proxies into latent space.
AI.method: GeoEdit uses a Lift-Manipulate-Render-Denoise pipeline: (1) decouple scene and object in 3D, (2) align via point correspondence, (3) render a geometry-aligned proxy with structural depth map, (4) Dual-Branch Denoising with a video diffusion backbone for object identity and variance-homogeneous injection of 3D constraints into foreground within a narrow denoising window, while background denoises freely.
AI.result: GeoEdit demonstrates consistent improvements in geometric accuracy, identity fidelity, and background quality compared to existing methods, validated on the introduced GeoEditBench benchmark with pose-aware evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: GeoEdit effectively solves the asymmetric problem of rigid object manipulation with free background synthesis by matching injected signal variance to native latent statistics, preventing self-attention disturbance.

title: SkelEM: Training-Signal Decoupling of Skeleton and Diffusion for Self-supervised Axial Super-Resolution in Volume Microscopy
summary: Volume microscopy, including electron and light microscopy, suffers from severe anisotropic resolution due to physical axial sectioning. Existing self-supervised axial super-resolution (ASR) methods face a trilemma bounded by overly smoothed regression textures, structural hallucinations of pure diffusion models, and prohibitive inference latency. In this paper, we propose Skeleton-refinE Microscopy (SkelEM), a self-supervised framework that decouples ASR at the training-signal level: a frozen topological network and a diffusion refiner are optimized by disjoint objectives, separating low-frequency topology formulation from high-frequency detail enhancement. Building on this deterministic skeleton, we exploit a unified cycle-consistent mechanism on input sparse slices to simultaneously extract a real-domain residual prior and bidirectionally align the diffusion refiner, washing away cross-plane artifacts without synthetic bias. By truncating the reverse diffusion process with this physical prior, SkelEM achieves high-fidelity detail restoration in merely $\le 5$ steps. To rigorously assess cross-instrument generalization, we further introduce BRAVE-ASR, a new benchmark of co-aligned anisotropic and isotropic volumes acquired on a Plasma-FIB instrument. Across public benchmarks, SkelEM achieves the most favorable balance across the fidelity-perception trade-off among self-supervised methods, with state-of-the-art downstream membrane segmentation performance and robust zero-shot generalization across distinct modalities.

AI.tldr: SkelEM is a self-supervised framework for axial super-resolution in volume microscopy that decouples low-frequency topology and high-frequency detail enhancement using a frozen topological network and a diffusion refiner with cycle-consistent mechanism, achieving high-fidelity restoration in ≤5 steps with state-of-the-art segmentation and zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Volume microscopy suffers from severe anisotropic resolution due to physical axial sectioning, and existing self-supervised ASR methods face a trilemma between overly smoothed regression textures, structural hallucinations of diffusion models, and prohibitive inference latency.
AI.method: SkelEM decouples ASR at the training-signal level using a frozen topological network and a diffusion refiner optimized by disjoint objectives. It employs a unified cycle-consistent mechanism on input sparse slices to extract real-domain residual prior and bidirectionally align the diffusion refiner, truncating reverse diffusion with this prior for ≤5 step restoration.
AI.result: SkelEM achieves the most favorable fidelity-perception balance among self-supervised methods, state-of-the-art membrane segmentation performance, robust zero-shot generalization across modalities, and introduces the BRAVE-ASR benchmark for cross-instrument evaluation.
AI.conclusion: SkelEM effectively resolves the trilemma in self-supervised axial super-resolution by decoupling topology and detail refinement, enabling fast, high-fidelity restoration with strong generalization across instruments and modalities.

title: Shell-Supervised Gaussian Splatting for Urban Real-to-Sim Reconstruction
comment: 10 pages main paper, 2 pages supplementary material
summary: Real-to-sim reconstruction for embodied AI requires geometry that is useful for collision reasoning, navigation, and agent-environment interaction, not only photorealistic novel-view synthesis. However, close-range urban facades are difficult for video-to-3D reconstruction: glass, reflections, repeated windows, and weak texture can produce visually plausible renderings with unstable surface geometry. We introduce shell-supervised Gaussian Splatting, a reconstruction-stage framework that uses an external facade structural shell as lightweight geometric supervision for video-driven Gaussian reconstruction. The method aligns an exterior shell to the video reconstruction frame, renders per-view depth, camera-space normal, and valid-mask maps, and applies these cues through mask-gated losses during Gaussian optimization. This design preserves RGB-driven appearance while regularizing only visible shell-supported facade regions. Experiments on anonymized close-range urban facade scenes show improved facade orientation and visible-surface point-cloud consistency over photo-only, monocular-cue, and surface-oriented Gaussian baselines, while maintaining comparable held-out rendering quality.

AI.tldr: Shell-supervised Gaussian Splatting uses lightweight facade structural shells as geometric supervision to improve 3D reconstruction of close-range urban facades, achieving better surface geometry while maintaining rendering quality.
AI.motivation: Close-range urban facades with glass, reflections, repeated windows, and weak texture are difficult for video-to-3D reconstruction, producing visually plausible renderings but unstable surface geometry.
AI.method: Introduces shell-supervised Gaussian Splatting: aligns an external facade structural shell to the video reconstruction, renders per-view depth, camera-space normal, and valid-mask maps, and applies mask-gated losses during Gaussian optimization to regularize only visible shell-supported facade regions.
AI.result: Improved facade orientation and visible-surface point-cloud consistency over photo-only, monocular-cue, and surface-oriented Gaussian baselines, while maintaining comparable held-out rendering quality.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight geometric supervision from structural shells effectively stabilizes facade geometry without compromising visual appearance, which is beneficial for embodied AI tasks requiring collision reasoning, navigation, and interaction.

title: Monte Carlo Energy Aggregation for Mobile 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Recent advances in 3D Gaussian Splatting have demonstrated unprecedented success in novel view synthesis. However, the substantial inference and storage overhead driven by high-order Spherical Harmonics (SH) are primary bottlenecks for mobile platforms. In this paper, we present Flux-GS, a real-time Gaussian Splatting method designed to achieve high-fidelity rendering with significantly reduced overhead for resource-constrained mobile platforms. We first propose a Monte Carlo Specular Energy Aggregator, sampling third-order radiance residuals and aggregating specular energy into a compact latent space. In this way, our method effectively preserves visually salient lighting features in lower-order bands without expensive distillation or pre-training. To mitigate the high-frequency details lost during compression, we introduce an Attribute-Conditioned SH Enhancement module. This module predicts Gaussian-aware offsets based on intrinsic Gaussian attributes, which enhance the first-order SH representation prior to inference, without extra inference costs. Furthermore, the original single-view gradient-based densification is prone to producing excessive Gaussians and overfitting to a certain view. We address these limitations by proposing a Multi-view Alpha-based Densification and Pruning strategy. By leveraging multi-view guidance, we ensure multi-view structure consistency and the precise removal of redundant primitives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Flux-GS achieves substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive visual quality, offering a robust and scalable solution for real-time mobile rendering. Code: \textcolor{magenta}{\href{https://xiaobiaodu.github.io/flux-gs-project/}{https://xiaobiaodu.github.io/flux-gs-project/}}.

AI.tldr: Flux-GS is a real-time 3D Gaussian Splatting method optimized for mobile platforms. It reduces overhead via a Monte Carlo Specular Energy Aggregator (compressing lighting into lower-order SH bands), an Attribute-Conditioned SH Enhancement module (restoring high-frequency details without extra inference cost), and Multi-view Alpha-based Densification/Pruning (reducing redundant Gaussians while ensuring multi-view consistency). It achieves competitive quality with far fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D Gaussian Splatting methods achieve high-quality novel view synthesis but suffer from high inference and storage overhead due to high-order Spherical Harmonics (SH), making them unsuitable for mobile platforms.
AI.method: (1) Monte Carlo Specular Energy Aggregator: samples third-order radiance residuals and aggregates specular energy into compact latent space. (2) Attribute-Conditioned SH Enhancement: predicts Gaussian-aware offsets to enhance first-order SH representation. (3) Multi-view Alpha-based Densification and Pruning: leverages multi-view guidance for consistent structure and removal of redundant Gaussians.
AI.result: Substantial parameter reduction while maintaining competitive visual quality; demonstrated in extensive experiments as a robust, scalable solution for real-time mobile rendering.
AI.conclusion: Flux-GS effectively balances rendering quality and computational/storage efficiency, offering a practical deployment solution for 3D Gaussian Splatting on resource-constrained mobile platforms.

title: OmniDance: Multimodal Driven Dance Video Generation with Large-scale Internet Data
summary: Music-driven dance video generation aims to synthesize expressive human motion that is temporally aligned with music while maintaining high visual fidelity. Despite recent progress, existing methods still face two key limitations: the lack of large-scale, high-quality dance video datasets, and the absence of principled frameworks for integrating music as a complementary conditioning signal into Video Generation Foundation Models. To address these limitations, we introduce CIPE-Dance, a large-scale Internet-sourced dance video dataset with choreography-informed text annotations, constructed via a progressive expert pipeline. To the best of our knowledge, CIPE-Dance is the largest dataset for dance video generation to date, comprising 300k high-quality clips over 400 hours and covering diverse dancers, environments, and dance genres. We further propose OmniDance, a framework-level recipe for integrating music into a TI2V foundation model without sacrificing its original controllability or visual fidelity. Motivated by the complementary roles of text as low-frequency semantics and music as high-frequency temporal dynamics, OmniDance co-designs a depth-aware specialization architecture, an anchored easy-to-hard curriculum learning strategy, and a modality-specialized time-dependent CFG strategy, enabling unified TI2V, MI2V, and MTI2V generation. Extensive experiments on CIPE-Dance demonstrate that OmniDance achieves state-of-the-art performance across all three tasks and exhibits robust multimodal integration capability. Project is available at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/OmniDance.

AI.tldr: CIPE-Dance (300K clips, 400 hrs) is the largest dance video dataset; OmniDance integrates music into text-conditioned video generation via depth-aware specialization, easy-to-hard curriculum learning, and modality-specialized CFG, achieving SOTA on TI2V, MI2V, and MTI2V tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing methods lack large-scale high-quality dance video datasets and principled frameworks for integrating music as a conditioning signal into Video Generation Foundation Models.
AI.method: (1) CIPE-Dance: large-scale dataset built via progressive expert pipeline with choreography-informed text annotations. (2) OmniDance: framework-level recipe with depth-aware specialization, anchored easy-to-hard curriculum learning, and modality-specialized time-dependent CFG strategy.
AI.result: OmniDance achieves state-of-the-art performance across text-to-video, music-to-video, and music-text-to-video generation tasks on CIPE-Dance.
AI.conclusion: The paper addresses dataset scarcity and missing music-conditioning frameworks for dance video generation, demonstrating robust multimodal integration capability.

title: Uncertainty Estimation in Pathology Foundation Models via Deep Mutual Learning
summary: Pathology foundation models (PFMs) offer generalizable representations for whole-slide image (WSI) analysis, yet their clinical adoption remains limited. Specifically, their predictions lack reliable confidence estimates, and no single PFM is universally best across tasks, which severely undermines trust in medical settings. To overcome this, we propose $\mathtt{DICE}$, a plug-and-play framework that ensembles $K$ frozen PFMs and models their disagreement as a proxy for uncertainty estimation. To ensure this proxy yields meaningful estimates, we align the ensemble members via deep mutual learning, and theoretically show that this objective upper-bounds the model uncertainty. Additionally, we demonstrate that the ensemble's consensus localizes abnormalities at the patch level without any explicit supervision. We evaluate $\mathtt{DICE}$ on three challenging WSI benchmarks. Notably, our framework provides reliable uncertainty estimates that accurately flag failure-prone cases under in- and out-of-distribution settings, while matching or outperforming SOTA baselines in classification, calibration, and localization. Overall, $\mathtt{DICE}$ takes a crucial step toward translating PFMs into uncertainty-aware decision-support systems.

AI.tldr: DICE is a plug-and-play framework that ensembles multiple frozen pathology foundation models (PFMs) with deep mutual learning to quantify uncertainty, improve classification, calibration, and enable unsupervised patch-level abnormality localization in whole-slide image analysis.
AI.motivation: Clinical adoption of PFMs is limited because they lack reliable confidence estimates and no single PFM is universally best across tasks, undermining trust in medical settings.
AI.method: DICE ensembles K frozen PFMs, models their disagreement as a proxy for uncertainty, aligns members via deep mutual learning (theoretically shown to upper-bound model uncertainty), and uses ensemble consensus for unsupervised patch-level abnormality localization.
AI.result: DICE provides reliable uncertainty estimates that flag failure-prone cases under in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, matching or outperforming SOTA baselines in classification, calibration, and localization on three WSI benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DICE takes a crucial step toward translating PFMs into uncertainty-aware decision-support systems for clinical use.

title: IBRSteG: Learning a Generalizable Steganography Framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Multimedia (TMM)
summary: Recent advances in deep learning have notably improved steganographic message hiding. However, designing a generalizable steganographic approach for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that can embed meaningful 3D scene content remains challenging. In this paper, we propose IBRSteG, a generalizable framework for 3DGS steganography that enables undetectable concealment of secret scenes within a steganographic scene. Unlike existing approaches whose parameter generation is rigidly coupled with the specific scene, we formulate 3D steganography as a feed-forward 3D Gaussian embedding process that generalizes across different 3DGS scenes. To realize this, we introduce GAS (Gaussian Attributes Steganographer), a network that learns a scene-independent embedding function by injecting the attributes of secret 3D Gaussian points into a cover scene, thereby directly reconstructing the steganographic scenes without per-scene finetuning or optimization. By transforming 3D Gaussian into these structured attributes, these attributes are compatible with 2D learning paradigms and benefit from their structured nature, thereby enhancing generalization to unseen 3DGS scenes. Extensive experiments on established datasets demonstrate that IBRSteG can effectively conceal different scenes with high visual quality, and achieves superior capacity and security. Code is available at https://github.com/LingXiang2023/IBRSteG.

AI.tldr: IBRSteG is a generalizable 3DGS steganography framework using a GAS network to embed secret 3D scenes into cover scenes via feed-forward Gaussian attribute injection, achieving undetectable hiding without per-scene optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing 3DGS steganography approaches require per-scene finetuning/optimization and lack generalization across different scenes; the authors aim to design a generalizable feed-forward framework that works across unseen 3DGS scenes.
AI.method: The GAS (Gaussian Attributes Steganographer) network learns a scene-independent embedding function by injecting secret 3D Gaussian attributes into a cover scene, converting 3D Gaussians into structured attributes compatible with 2D learning paradigms for direct steganographic scene reconstruction.
AI.result: IBRSteG achieves effective concealment of various scenes with high visual quality, superior capacity, and security, as demonstrated on established datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed generalizable framework overcomes the rigidity of prior per-scene methods and enables scalable, undetectable 3D scene steganography with strong performance.

title: MuseBench: Benchmarking Intent-Level Audiovisual Arts Understanding in MLLMs
summary: Audiovisual arts encompass diverse creative disciplines, including cinema, visual arts, stage performance, and game design, where artistic meaning arises from deliberate combinations of visual, auditory, and narrative elements (e.g., fear amplified through claustrophobic framing, or grief conveyed through silence and lingering close-ups). True artistic understanding extends beyond recognizing what is depicted to reasoning about why it is expressed through particular creative choices. Despite the strong progress of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), this critical aspect of artistic understanding remains underexplored, as existing benchmarks largely measure perceptual recognition while overlooking reasoning about creative intent. To address this gap, we introduce Musebench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate MLLMs on nuanced artistic understanding. It comprises 4,016 questions spanning cinematic arts, static visual arts, stage performing arts, and game arts, distilled from over 10K candidate video essays that pair professional commentary with visual demonstration. To capture the open-ended nature of artistic analysis at scale, the benchmark combines single-select and variable-option multi-select questions. All questions are generated and refined through a four-phase iterative pipeline combining shortcut filtering, adversarial distractors, and expert validation. Comprehensive zero-shot evaluation of 28 state-of-the-art MLLMs reveals that even the best-performing model achieves only 48.29% accuracy, substantially below human expert performance of 87.18%, exposing a significant gap in current models' creative domain expertise.

AI.tldr: Musebench is a new benchmark (4,016 questions across cinema, visual arts, stage performance, and game design) evaluating MLLMs on reasoning about creative intent in audiovisual arts. Even the best model achieved only 48.29% accuracy vs. human expert 87.18%, revealing a major gap in current models' artistic understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLM benchmarks focus on perceptual recognition (what is depicted) but overlook reasoning about creative intent (why it is expressed through particular choices). True artistic understanding requires analyzing the deliberate combinations of visual, auditory, and narrative elements.
AI.method: 4,016 questions distilled from 10K+ video essays across four art domains (cinema, static visual arts, stage performing arts, game arts). Questions combine single-select and variable-option multi-select formats. Generated via a four-phase iterative pipeline: shortcut filtering, adversarial distractors, and expert validation.
AI.result: Zero-shot evaluation of 28 state-of-the-art MLLMs: best model achieved only 48.29% accuracy, significantly below human expert performance of 87.18%.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs have a substantial gap in creative domain expertise for artistic understanding, highlighting the need for benchmarks like Musebench that evaluate reasoning about creative intent rather than just perceptual recognition.

title: Cross-Modal Iteration Distillation for Robust IHD Screening: The IDNet Framework and A New Benchmark
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics (SMC 2026)
summary: Color Fundus Photography (CFP) offers a low-cost and non-invasive route for ischemic heart disease (IHD) screening, but current studies are limited by scarce public benchmarks and ineffective fusion of retinal images with sparse clinical variables. We propose IDNet, a multimodal framework with a Cross-Modal Distillation Aggregator (CDA) that uses learnable queries to sequentially integrate left-eye, right-eye, and clinical features, mitigating the imbalance between high-dimensional visual features and low-dimensional tabular inputs. We also construct a reproducible UK Biobank benchmark with open-source curation and quality-control pipelines, yielding 50,410 images from 25,205 subjects. On this benchmark, IDNet outperforms image-only, clinical-only, and several multimodal baselines, and CDA consistently improves multiple visual encoders as a plug-in fusion module.

AI.tldr: IDNet is a multimodal framework for ischemic heart disease screening from color fundus photos, using a Cross-Modal Distillation Aggregator (CDA) to fuse left-eye, right-eye, and clinical features, achieving state-of-the-art results on a new UK Biobank benchmark.
AI.motivation: CFP offers low-cost IHD screening, but current studies lack public benchmarks and effective fusion of retinal images with sparse clinical variables.
AI.method: IDNet uses a Cross-Modal Distillation Aggregator (CDA) with learnable queries to sequentially integrate left-eye, right-eye, and clinical features, balancing high-dimensional visual data with low-dimensional tabular inputs.
AI.result: On a 50,410-image UK Biobank benchmark, IDNet outperforms image-only, clinical-only, and multimodal baselines; CDA consistently improves multiple visual encoders as a plug-in fusion module.
AI.conclusion: IDNet with CDA effectively addresses multimodal imbalance for IHD screening from CFP, and the reproducible benchmark supports future research.

title: CogSENet: Blind Image Deblurring with Blur-Conditioned Semantic Routing and Explicit Frequency Fusion
summary: Blind image deblurring demands the recovery of high-fidelity details and coherent structures from complex, unknown degradations. Current blind image deblurring methods struggle with real-world, spatially varying degradations, and lack the semantic awareness necessary to reliably differentiate valid textures from artifacts. To bridge this gap, we propose CogSENet, a dynamic, semantic-aligned reconstruction framework inspired by the eagle's visual system. By mimicking the eagle's active saccadic scanning, we devise a Semantic-Driven State Space Module (SDSSM) with semantic-aware token regrouping via differentiable routing, enabling prompt-conditioned long-range dependency modeling. To ensure physically interpretable recovery of textures and structures, a BiFreqFusionBlock (BFFB) mirrors functional differentiation of the eagle's retina by decomposing features into high and low frequencies using wavelet transforms. Finally, we estimate a continuous Blur Field (CBF) from blur image and fuse it with CLIP semantic priors to modulate the deepest latent features, emulating focal adaptation and enabling adaptive restoration under spatially non-uniform blur. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CogSENetoutperforms state-of-the-art deblurring methods in both visual quality and structural fidelity with fewer parameters, while also performing favorably on dehazing, deraining, and denoising tasks.

AI.tldr: CogSENet is a novel blind image deblurring framework inspired by the eagle's visual system, using semantic-aware token regrouping (SDSSM), frequency decomposition (BFFB), and CLIP-guided continuous blur field estimation to achieve state-of-the-art deblurring with fewer parameters, and generalizes to dehazing, deraining, and denoising.
AI.motivation: Current blind image deblurring methods fail to handle real-world spatially varying degradations and lack semantic awareness to distinguish valid textures from artifacts.
AI.method: CogSENet (1) Semantic-Driven State Space Module (SDSSM) for token regrouping and long-range dependency modeling; (2) BiFreqFusionBlock (BFFB) for wavelet-based frequency decomposition; (3) Continuous Blur Field (CBF) estimation fused with CLIP semantic priors for adaptive restoration.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art deblurring methods in visual quality and structural fidelity with fewer parameters; also performs favorably on dehazing, deraining, and denoising tasks.
AI.conclusion: Bio-inspired design (eagle's visual system) combined with semantic-aware and frequency-decomposed modeling provides a parameter-efficient, generalizable solution for blind image restoration.

title: Consensus Clustering of Free-Viewing Gaze Data: New Insights into Human-Information Interaction
summary: Free-viewing gaze data provides a rich, task-free window into human visual attention. Conventional exploratory data analysis of the data provides user attention patterns through fixations and areas of interest. However, despite the richness of this gaze data, its human-information interaction (HII) patterns are understudied. We address this gap using consensus clustering of gaze data with respect to users and stimulus characteristics. We present a novel end-to-end unsupervised ensemble learning system for consensus clustering of free-viewing gaze datasets, EnsembleGaze. With a goal of characterizing the user behavior and stimulus type, we propose a feature engineering step based on statistical descriptors of fixation-based distributions. EnsembleGaze involves consensus voting of selected clustering methods implemented on the feature vector to compute the co-association matrix. Using the separate consensus clustering of users and stimuli as a baseline, we further propose two high-dimensional clustering strategies for determining gaze clusters based on joint user and image characterization. They are consensus subspace clustering and spectral biclustering. Clustering performance is evaluated using selected standard metrics and is further interpreted through image-level properties. Our system provides a replicable method for the unsupervised analysis of fixation behavior in scene perception research. Our results show that image stimuli groupings are highly consistent across methods, reflecting a robust ambient-versus-focal viewing mode distinction, whereas user groupings are image-context-dependent, a structure that only biclustering and the two-step conditional approaches are architecturally capable of recovering. Testing on the publicly available datasets revealed dataset-specific patterns, with each offering complementary insights through distinct clustering strategies.

AI.tldr: EnsembleGaze is an unsupervised ensemble learning system for clustering free-viewing gaze data, revealing robust stimulus groupings (ambient vs. focal viewing) and context-dependent user groupings through consensus clustering, subspace clustering, and spectral biclustering methods.
AI.motivation: Free-viewing gaze data is rich but understudied in terms of human-information interaction (HII) patterns. Existing exploratory analyses focus on fixations and AOIs but lack systematic unsupervised characterization of both user behavior and stimulus types.
AI.method: The system uses statistical descriptors of fixation distributions for feature engineering, applies consensus clustering (voting across methods) to build a co-association matrix, and proposes two high-dimensional clustering strategies: consensus subspace clustering and spectral biclustering for joint user-image characterization.
AI.result: Image stimuli groupings are highly consistent across methods, revealing a robust ambient-vs-focal viewing distinction. User groupings are image-context-dependent, recoverable only by biclustering and two-step conditional approaches. Dataset-specific patterns emerged across public datasets.
AI.conclusion: EnsembleGaze provides a replicable unsupervised method for analyzing fixation behavior in scene perception, highlighting that stimulus-based clustering is stable while user clustering depends on image context—a structure best captured by biclustering approaches.

title: Walking in the Implicit: Interactive World Exploration via Neural Scene Representation
summary: Interactive video generation systems for camera-controlled world exploration roll out growing sequences of latent video frames, entangling state transition with high-frequency observation synthesis. We propose Walking in the Implicit, a scene-centric paradigm that changes the rollout variable from frame latents to a fixed-length, renderable implicit state, termed Neural Implicit Scene (NIS). This factorizes interactive generation into stochastic transition of a compact scene state and deterministic pose-conditioned rendering given the sampled state. We instantiate this paradigm as NeuWorld: a transformer VAE learns locally anchored NIS from sparse posed frames, and a diffusion transformer evolves NIS conditioned on future camera trajectories and geometry-aware retrieved history. By reusing the VAE encoder as a unified conditioner, NeuWorld maps camera, reference-image, and history cues into the same NIS modality, avoiding external heterogeneous encoders. Trained from scratch on public posed-view data without pretrained video backbones or auxiliary 3D reconstructors, NeuWorld achieves strong long-horizon consistency with favorable inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: NeuWorld introduces Neural Implicit Scene (NIS) as a fixed-length renderable state for interactive video generation, decoupling scene evolution from frame rendering, achieving long-horizon consistency and efficiency without pretrained backbones.
AI.motivation: Existing interactive video generation entangles state transitions with high-frequency observation synthesis, leading to inconsistency and inefficiency in long-horizon generation.
AI.method: Proposes scene-centric paradigm using Neural Implicit Scene (NIS) as compact state. NeuWorld uses transformer VAE to learn NIS from sparse posed frames, and diffusion transformer to evolve NIS conditioned on future trajectories and geometry-aware history. VAE encoder unifies camera, reference-image, and history conditioning.
AI.result: Achieves strong long-horizon consistency with favorable inference efficiency, trained from scratch on public posed-view data without pretrained video backbones or 3D reconstructors.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling scene state transition from rendering via implicit neural scene representation improves long-horizon interactive video generation quality and efficiency.

title: Argus: Metric Panoramic 3D Reconstruction for Indoor Scenes
summary:
Metric feed-forward 3D reconstruction for panoramic data remains under-explored due to the lack of large-scale panoramic RGB-D training data.
  We present Realsee3D, a hybrid dataset of 10K indoor scenes (1K real, 9K synthetic) with 299K panoramic viewpoints and precise metric annotations,
  and Argus, a feed-forward network trained on it for metric panoramic 3D reconstruction.
  In the sparse unordered capture setting of Realsee3D,
  a poorly chosen coordinate anchor can cause global pose drift.
  Argus addresses this with a learned covisibility module that selects the geometrically optimal reference view to anchor the metric world frame.
  To further improve multi-task learning,
  we decompose the bidirectional pixel-to-world mapping into interpretable sub-steps with per-step supervision and cross-coordinate joint constraints,
  reinforcing geometric consistency across prediction branches.
  On the Realsee3D benchmark,
  Argus achieves state-of-the-art metric performance in camera pose estimation, depth estimation, and point cloud reconstruction.
  Project page: https://argus-paper.realsee.ai.

AI.tldr: Realsee3D (10K indoor scenes with 299K panoramic views) and Argus network achieve SOTA metric panoramic 3D reconstruction using a learned covisibility module and decomposed bidirectional pixel-to-world mapping with cross-coordinate constraints.
AI.motivation: Metric feed-forward 3D reconstruction for panoramic data is under-explored due to the lack of large-scale panoramic RGB-D training data. Also, sparse unordered capture settings suffer from global pose drift caused by poorly chosen coordinate anchors.
AI.method: Proposes Realsee3D (1K real + 9K synthetic indoor scenes, 299K panoramic viewpoints with metric annotations) and Argus network featuring: (1) a learned covisibility module to select optimal reference view for metric world frame anchoring; (2) decomposed bidirectional pixel-to-world mapping with per-step supervision and cross-coordinate joint constraints.
AI.result: State-of-the-art metric performance on camera pose estimation, depth estimation, and point cloud reconstruction on the Realsee3D benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Combining a large-scale hybrid panoramic dataset with a carefully designed feed-forward network (covisibility-guided anchoring + decomposed pixel-to-world mapping) enables high-quality metric panoramic 3D reconstruction.

title: Bridging the Gap Between Image Restoration and Navigational Safety in Hazy Conditions: A New Visibility Estimation Metric for Maritime Surveillance
summary: Visibility distance is critical to maritime navigational safety because it determines the effective observation range of shipborne and shore-based monitoring systems. Under hazy conditions, degraded visual information shortens observable distance and increases navigational risks and economic losses. Although numerous image dehazing methods have been developed, conventional image quality assessment metrics, such as PSNR, SSIM, FSIM, FADE, and NIQE, cannot establish a physically interpretable relationship between restoration quality and practical visibility thresholds. To address this limitation, this work proposes a visibility-oriented evaluation framework that links dehazing performance with visible-distance estimation. First, a Maritime Simulated Visibility Dataset (MSVD) is constructed using Unity3D to simulate maritime traffic scenes under graded visibility conditions. The dataset provides paired hazy and clear images with precise visibility annotations, enabling quantitative analysis of visibility restoration. Second, a dehazing visibility evaluation metric is developed by using object detection accuracy as an intermediate indicator. By establishing a mapping between visibility distance and detection performance, the proposed metric converts image restoration improvements into measurable visibility gains. Six representative dehazing methods are evaluated using both conventional image quality metrics and the proposed visibility metric. Experimental results under different imaging conditions demonstrate that MSVD provides a reliable benchmark for evaluating dehazing performance across graded visibility levels, while the proposed metric enables interpretable and reliable visible-distance estimation, thereby supporting the assessment of navigational safety and operational efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a visibility-oriented evaluation framework for maritime image dehazing, using a simulated dataset (MSVD) and object detection accuracy to link restoration quality with practical visibility distance estimation, overcoming limitations of conventional metrics.
AI.motivation: Conventional image quality metrics (PSNR, SSIM, etc.) cannot establish a physically interpretable relationship between dehazing quality and practical visibility thresholds, which is critical for maritime navigational safety.
AI.method: (1) Constructs MSVD using Unity3D with graded visibility annotations; (2) Develops a dehazing visibility evaluation metric using object detection accuracy as an intermediate indicator to map restoration improvements to visibility gains.
AI.result: MSVD provides a reliable benchmark for graded visibility evaluation; the proposed metric enables interpretable visible-distance estimation across different imaging conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed visibility-oriented framework supports navigational safety and operational efficiency assessment by linking dehazing performance to measurable visibility distance.

title: Illuminating Unified Multimodal Model for Free-form Interleaved Text-Image Generation
summary: The advancement of generative AI models capable of producing text and image marks a critical step forward in the realm of multimodal intelligence, particularly for tasks involving the interleaving of both modalities. To advance this intelligence to the next stage, it is crucial for models to autonomously generate free-form interleaved text-image sequences. In this paper, we introduce ILLUME-X, an advanced unified multimodal paradigm that enables high-quality, free-form interleaved text-image generation by improving multimodal data efficiency and stabilizing the multimodal training process. ILLUME-X comprises three key components: (i) an expanded training data pipeline optimized for interleaved text-image generation, (ii) a progressive training strategy with self-adaptive objectives for free-length multimodal token sequences, and (iii) an objective and comprehensive evaluation method ILScore for interleaved text-image sequences. Notably, our ILLUME-X outperforms previous unified models across multiple interleaved text-image generation tasks like style transfer, image decomposition and storytelling.

AI.tldr: ILLUME-X is a unified multimodal model that improves free-form interleaved text-image generation through better data pipelines, progressive training strategies, and a new evaluation method (ILScore), outperforming prior unified models in tasks like style transfer, image decomposition, and storytelling.
AI.motivation: To advance multimodal AI to the next stage, models must autonomously generate free-form interleaved text-image sequences, which existing approaches still struggle with due to data inefficiency and training instability.
AI.method: ILLUME-X introduces three components: (1) an expanded training data pipeline optimized for interleaved text-image generation, (2) a progressive training strategy with self-adaptive objectives for free-length multimodal token sequences, and (3) an objective evaluation method called ILScore for assessing interleaved text-image sequences.
AI.result: ILLUME-X outperforms previous unified models across multiple interleaved text-image generation tasks, including style transfer, image decomposition, and storytelling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed paradigm effectively addresses key challenges in multimodal data efficiency and training stability, leading to superior performance in free-form interleaved text-image generation.

title: Emergence of a Shared Canonical Object Frame from In-the-Wild Videos
summary: Comparing object orientations and positions across different instances requires their poses to be expressed in a shared canonical frame. Establishing such frames has traditionally required manual annotation, creating a scaling bottleneck that limits category and instance diversity. We show that a shared canonical frame can instead emerge from self-supervised training on object-centric videos captured in the wild, using only noisy camera poses from Structure-from-Motion. Our key idea is to route all training sequences through a shared geometric bottleneck: a coarse canonical mesh that carries no category-specific detail. By learning dense correspondences from image pixels to this mesh, and estimating per-sequence alignments from noisy SfM geometry, a common canonical frame emerges from multi-view consistency and the semantic priors of the feature extractor, without any canonical pose labels or category conditioning. Trained in a self-supervised manner on 160,000 in-the-wild object videos, our method achieves competitive accuracy on category-level pose estimation benchmarks compared to methods that rely on canonical pose supervision. The code and checkpoint is available on https://github.com/Fischer-Tom/Emergent-Canonical-Frame/.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised method that learns a shared canonical frame from in-the-wild object videos using noisy SfM poses and a shared coarse mesh, achieving competitive category-level pose estimation without manual annotations.
AI.motivation: Traditional canonical frame establishment requires manual annotation, creating a scaling bottleneck that limits category and instance diversity. The goal is to remove this annotation dependency.
AI.method: Uses self-supervised training on 160,000 in-the-wild object videos with noisy camera poses from SfM. A shared coarse canonical mesh serves as a geometric bottleneck, and dense correspondences from image pixels to this mesh are learned. Per-sequence alignments are estimated from noisy SfM geometry, allowing a common canonical frame to emerge from multi-view consistency and semantic priors.
AI.result: Achieves competitive accuracy on category-level pose estimation benchmarks compared to methods that rely on canonical pose supervision.
AI.conclusion: Shared canonical frames can emerge purely from self-supervised learning on diverse, unlabeled object videos, eliminating the need for manual canonical pose annotation and enabling scalability to more categories and instances.

title: Clinical Risk-Aware Multi-Level Grading for Coronary Artery Stenosis through Curved Feature Reconstruction
summary: Developing a multi-level grading model for coronary artery stenosis holds great clinical significance for the diagnosis of coronary artery disease. However, designing an effective multi-level deep learning algorithm faces significant challenges. Specifically, utilizing CCTA or 3D SCPR images alone presents inherent shortcomings: CCTA images are difficult to analyze due to the tortuous paths of blood vessels, while 3D SCPR images are prone to abnormal distortions that hinder accurate grading. Furthermore, different stenosis grades are associated with varying clinical risks, and incorporating this association into the algorithm is non-trivial. To address the former problems, we propose the Curved Feature Reconstruction (CFR) module, which uses vessel curves as prior and employs a point-by-point correspondence strategy to precisely align and fuse features from both 3D SCPR and CCTA images. Meanwhile, a Clinical Risk-Aware (CR) Loss is employed to introduce clinical risk relevance into the network training so that the algorithm can better align with the clinical diagnosis. The experimental results on a in-house dataset reveal that our approach significantly outperforms other methods, and several ablation studies also demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed designs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a multi-level grading model for coronary artery stenosis that fuses CCTA and 3D SCPR images using a Curved Feature Reconstruction module and a Clinical Risk-Aware Loss, achieving superior performance on an in-house dataset.
AI.motivation: Multi-level grading of coronary artery stenosis is clinically crucial for diagnosing coronary artery disease, but existing approaches using either CCTA or 3D SCPR images alone suffer from inherent limitations—CCTA has tortuous vessel paths making analysis difficult, while 3D SCPR images are prone to abnormal distortions.
AI.method: 1) Curved Feature Reconstruction (CFR) module that uses vessel curves as priors and a point-by-point correspondence strategy to align and fuse features from both 3D SCPR and CCTA images. 2) Clinical Risk-Aware (CR) Loss that incorporates clinical risk relevance into network training to better align with clinical diagnosis.
AI.result: The proposed method significantly outperformed other methods on an in-house dataset. Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of the proposed CFR module and CR Loss.
AI.conclusion: Fusing multi-modal CCTA and 3D SCPR features with clinical risk awareness is an effective strategy for improving multi-level coronary artery stenosis grading.

title: One Forward Beats Two: InnerZoom for Accurate and Efficient GUI Grounding
summary: MLLM-based GUI grounding methods commonly formulate target localization as autoregressive coordinate generation, enabling models to leverage the strong instruction-following and semantic understanding capabilities of MLLMs. However, this formulation requires the model to retain region-level target evidence while decoding coordinate tokens with the spatial precision demanded by GUI clicking. Our diagnostic analysis reveals that target-region awareness emerges in intermediate decoder layers but is neither retained nor translated into the final coordinate prediction. Existing ZoomIn-style methods address this issue through an external crop-and-rerun pass, which improves localization but increases end-to-end latency and computational cost. To retain the accuracy benefits of two-pass zooming without this extra cost, we propose InnerZoom, a single-forward framework for cross-layer evidence bridging. InnerZoom transforms target-related cues from the original forward pass into a compact cross-layer evidence state, then preserves, refines, and reinjects this state throughout later decoding layers to guide coordinate prediction. Extensive experimental results suggest that InnerZoom-4B achieves state-of-the-art performance on all six GUI grounding benchmarks, obtaining 64.7 on OSWorld-G, 40.2 on UI-Vision, 73.1 on OSWorld-GR, and 87.6 on MMBench-GUI, surpassing the previous best results by 4.1, 3.2, 2.9, and 2.3 points, respectively. Under a controlled 4B setting, InnerZoom improves the same SFT+RL baseline by 5.3 points on average and outperforms two-pass ZoomIn by 1.3 points on average, while reducing end-to-end latency by up to 31.8% and TFLOPs by about 29%. Code and models will be publicly available.
